Massachusetts Task Force 1 - MA-TF1
Technical Search Field Manual
/
Manual Home
Section 05

Search Segmentation & Assignments

Dividing the search area into manageable segments, assigning teams, tracking coverage, and ensuring effective handoff between operational periods.

Section 05 · Search Segmentation & Assignments

Segmentation Basics

Effective segmentation ensures complete coverage, prevents duplication, and provides clear team accountability. Segments are defined before operations begin.

  • Divide the total search area into logical, manageable units (by structure, floor, grid sector, or area)
  • Each segment assigned a unique designator (alpha-numeric: A1, B2, etc.)
  • Segment boundaries defined by physical features where possible (walls, streets, structural lines)
  • Segment size should not exceed a 2-person team's capacity for one operational period
  • All segments entered into SARCOP before teams deploy

Assignment Breakdown

Each team assignment must contain the following elements before the team departs.

  • Team designator and member names
  • Assigned segment(s)
  • Search type (hasty / primary / secondary / technical)
  • Coverage level required
  • Equipment assigned (tech tools, medical kit)
  • Radio channel and check-in schedule
  • Time assigned and expected return
  • Egress route and safety zone location

Assignment Communications

  • Assignment communicated verbally and confirmed by Team Leader read-back
  • Written assignment card or SARCOP task issued to Team Leader
  • Check-in every 30 minutes minimum on tactical channel
  • Status updates: "Negative contact," "Positive indicator — [location]," "Segment complete"
  • Any change in assignment communicated to Search Branch immediately

Structure Based Assignments

Used when search area consists of identifiable structures. Each structure or floor is assigned as a discrete segment.

  • Designate by building number/address and floor: e.g., Bldg 3 — Floor 2
  • Include structure type, collapse pattern, and hazard notes in assignment
  • Stairwells and mechanical spaces are separate segments
  • Mark structure with FEMA survey mark on assignment and physical structure

Area Based Assignments

Used for open debris fields, multi-block incidents, or when structural boundaries are undefined.

  • Grid overlay applied to map; each cell is a segment
  • Cell size determined by debris density and team capability
  • Boundary coords entered in SARCOP / Field Maps
  • GPS waypoints used to confirm segment boundaries in field

Coverage Tracking

  • Each segment status tracked in SARCOP: Unassigned / Assigned / In Progress / Complete / Needs Re-check
  • Physical FEMA marks applied to structures upon completion
  • Coverage map updated in real-time by Documentation Unit
  • Branch Director reviews coverage map at each operational period boundary

Handoff Notes

Handoff occurs at end of each operational period or team rotation. All information must be documented before relief team deploys.

  • Segments searched and coverage level achieved
  • Positive indicators and locations — with GPS or sketch
  • Active hazards identified — including changes since initial size-up
  • Equipment left in place or deployed in structure
  • Pending actions for relief team
  • SARCOP updated and documentation transferred to oncoming Documentation Unit
Section 06

Listening Devices

Section 06 · Listening Devices
Section 06 — Listening Devices
Listening Search Devices

Listening search devices detect victim-generated signals — voice, knocking, scratching, movement — transmitted through structural elements and air. They are deployed by Technical Search Specialists (TSS) under All Quiet conditions to detect and triangulate victim location in structural collapse environments.

Built from FEMA TSS ILT Module 1 (Ver. 2.1, Feb 2023). Sources: FEMA US&R Rescue Specialist Training Manual App. A · Fire Engineering · Firehouse · Savox · Leader Group.

Critical Limitation

Listening devices cannot detect an unconscious victim who cannot generate a signal. Canine or other technology (UWB radar, thermal) is required to find non-responsive victims.

Key Principle

No single search method is sufficient alone. Combine listening, canine, and visual confirmation for every find.

Module Contents

1. What is Sound
2. How Sensors Work
3. Victim Call-Out Protocol
4. Detecting vs. Locating
5. Sensor Placement Patterns
6. Sensor Placement Field Rules
7. Surface Sound Conductivity
8. Structure Type Strategy
9. Interference Management
10. Search Task Sequence
11. Equipment Handling
12. Acoustic vs. Seismic Sensors
13. Detection Range Reference
14. Signs of Life Hierarchy
15. Where Listening Devices Fit
16. Advanced Operations
17. Power Sources & Filtering
18. Knowledge Check & Summary

What is Sound?

Sound is vibration transmitted through a medium. In US&R, detecting any signal from a victim — calling, crying, knocking, breathing, or even heartbeat vibration — can mean the difference between life and death.

Sound Properties

Wavelength

Distance between equivalent points on consecutive cycles — the physical length of the wave.

Amplitude

Strength / power of the signal. Higher = louder. The closest sensor to the source shows the largest amplitude.

Frequency (kHz)

Cycles per second. Higher = higher pitch. High frequencies attenuate fastest with distance.

Sound Transmission Types

Airborne Sound

Ears are the receivers — wide dynamic range. Limited when victims are entombed. Sound travels INTO rubble easier than OUT.

Structure-Borne Sound

Requires piezo-dielectric sensors to convert vibration to signal. Modern sensors read amplitude, not time delay.

How Sensors Work

Core Principle

A piezo-dielectric sensor acts like a tiny seismograph. Any vibration on the housing causes the internal mass to move relative to the casing, generating a voltage. That voltage is amplified, filtered, and sent to headphones or a visual display — converting invisible structure-borne vibration into something the operator can hear and see.

Signal Points of Failure

  1. Sensor Body
    Physical damage to housing degrades or eliminates signal conversion.
  2. Connecting Cable & Pins
    Most fragile and most commonly damaged component. Treat as mission-critical.
  3. Main Monitor Unit
    Amplifier and display — damage here affects all connected sensors.
  4. Power Supply / Battery
    Depleted power causes signal degradation before full loss.
  5. Headphones
    Damaged drivers reduce operator's ability to detect weak signals.
  6. User Interpretation
    Operator fatigue and noise discipline failures are the final failure point.

Equipment Requirements

  • Sturdy — must survive collapse site conditions
  • Easy to operate under stress with PPE
  • Free from electrical interference
  • Visual display for low-frequency signals
  • Multiple sensor inputs for triangulation

Victim Call-Out Protocol

The 3-Knock Standard
"ANYONE IN THIS STRUCTURE — KNOCK THREE TIMES!"

Structure-borne knocking travels far better through rubble than an airborne voice. Humans are natural mimics — a conscious victim will typically reply with 3 knocks at the same rhythm. Three knocks is a universally recognized distress signal.

Ask to Knock, Not Yell

Structure-borne knocking is far more detectable than an airborne voice from an entombed victim.

When Airborne Fails

Knock 3× with a heavy rock or mallet against a pipe or structural element on the outside to send signal inward.

Victim Fatigue

Knocking is exhausting. Always tell the victim to rest while you reposition sensors.

Detecting vs. Locating

Critical Distinction

Detection confirms a victim exists. Locating pinpoints exactly where. Treat them as separate, sequential phases — do not skip from detection directly to rescue entry.

Detection Mode
Goal

Confirm a living victim exists somewhere in the structure.

  • Sensor on a pipe is acceptable — location will be vague
  • 1–2 sensors sufficient for rapid sweep
  • Success here = switch to Locating Mode
Locating Mode
Goal

Pinpoint victim location for rescue entry.

  • Compare amplitude — loudest = closest sensor
  • Near source = sharp/crisp; far = muffled
  • All sensors: same material, same orientation

Sensor Placement Patterns

Triangulation strategies for 2, 3, and 4 sensors. Amplitude comparison — not time delay — is how modern devices localize a source.

2 Sensors — Equal Loudness

Source lies along the perpendicular bisector of the line between the two sensors. Reposition one sensor perpendicular to narrow the location.

2 Sensors — Unequal Loudness

Source is closer to the louder sensor. Circle around the louder sensor to find the point of maximum amplitude.

3 Sensors — Triangulation

Intersection point of the amplitude comparison lines identifies victim location. Most reliable field method for locating.

4 Sensors — Block Pattern

If all four sensors show equal amplitude, victim is at the center of the block. Equal amplitude on closely-spaced sensors may also indicate a large source farther away.

Spacing Warning

Do NOT place sensors too close together. Equal amplitude across closely-spaced sensors may indicate a large source much farther away. Recommended spacing: 16–33 ft (5–10 m) depending on surface. One sensor (the louder/clearer) should always remain in place while repositioning others.

Sensor Placement — Field Rules

Surface Prep & Attachment

  • Clean the surface — remove gravel, dirt, carpet, insulation
  • Firm contact required — sensor weight often sufficient
  • Use clamps, straps, duct tape, or steel spikes as needed
  • Magnets ideal for steel beams and pipes
  • Weigh down cables to prevent wind-induced vibration

Placement Strategy

  • Prefer solid structure — foundations, walls, intact slabs
  • Pipes: great for detection; unreliable for locating
  • Avoid resonating surfaces — thin boards, exposed I-beams
  • Isolated rubble with no structural connection = no signal
  • Same material + same orientation for all sensors when locating

Surface Sound Conductivity

Surface material determines how far a signal travels and how clearly it arrives. Match placement strategy to the dominant surface type at your site.

SurfaceConductivitySignal CharacterOperational Notes
Steel Pipe / BeamEXCELLENTClear, high-freq ringBest for detection. Pipes meander — don't use for final locating.
Concrete — Intact SlabGOODRings, lower tone than steelBest surface for sensor comparison. Ideal for pancake collapses.
Concrete — BrokenMODERATEMuffled, loses high freqPoor contact between pieces degrades transfer significantly.
Wood — IntactMODERATEMuffled, dullCall-out / listen may outperform seismic sensors in wood-frame buildings.
Masonry / BrickMODERATEVariable by conditionUse intact wall sections only.
Soft Soil / SandPOORVery short rangeDecrease sensor spacing significantly.

Structure Type — Search Strategy

Structure type determines the relative effectiveness of call-out, seismic sensors, and canine. Select method mix based on dominant construction type at the site.

Building TypeCall-OutSeismic SensorsCanineKey Notes
Light Frame (Wood) EFFECTIVE LIMITED POSSIBLE Broken wood = poor vibration transmitter.
Heavy Wall (URM/Tilt-Up) POSSIBLE MODERATE POSSIBLE Broken masonry + collapsed floors are poor transmitters.
Concrete Frame LIMITED MOST EFFECTIVE DIRECTIONAL Seismic excels with intact pancaked slabs.
Precast Concrete POSSIBLE VARIABLE VARIABLE Depends on void size and structural interconnection.

Interference Management

Rule #1

Always listen FIRST in unfiltered, wide-band mode. Never apply filters until completing an initial unfiltered pass — scratching, breathing, or crying signals may be missed.

Natural Interference

  • Wind: place sensors inside structure, weigh down cables
  • Rain / hail: shield sensor, decrease spacing to compensate
  • Water flow: address immediately — flooding / secondary collapse risk
  • Sun / heat: thermal creak / pop is normal structural noise, not a victim signal

Human-Made Interference

  • Jackhammer: MUST shut down during listening — most disruptive source
  • Diamond saw: filter high end — knocking still detectable
  • Machinery: all ops on connected structure must pause during listening window
  • Aircraft: low-freq ground vibration, hard to filter — wait for aircraft to clear

Search Task Sequence

The standard 10-phase search task sequence for a listening device deployment. Follow in order — each phase informs the next.

#PhaseAction
1Size UpWalk site. ID hazards, visible victims, road access. Draw sketch map.
2Witness IntelTalk to witnesses. How many inside? Neighbors home? Medical needs?
3Structure TypeID collapse type (V, lean-to, pancake, mine shaft, buried vehicle) and material.
4Check VoidsQuick check of accessible voids for immediate finds and rapid extrication.
5Call OutHail victims (multi-language). Request 3 knocks. Knock pipes if airborne fails.
6Rapid Search1–2 sensors on major structural members. Detect first, then locate.
7Comms ProtocolSet evacuation signals, radio freq, hand signals. Coordinate with IC / Safety.
8Assign DivisionsSeparate TSS search from rescue ops when possible for parallel coverage.
9Grid SearchSystematic full-area coverage. Mark sectors cleared with paint / flags.
10Hand OffSketch map all findings. Share intel with incoming teams and the ICP.

Equipment Handling

Most Critical Component

Cable connector pins are the most fragile and most commonly damaged component. Treat cables as mission-critical. Signal chain: Sensor → Cable → Monitor → Power Supply → Headphones → User.

Pre-Deployment Checks

  • Battery life confirmed — know run time on full charge
  • Huddle test / diagnostics completed
  • TF system checkout procedure completed
  • All sensors, cables, headphones accounted for
  • Connector pins inspected — most fragile component
  • Cable condition checked — no kinks, cuts, fraying

Post-Deployment / Storage

  • Clean equipment before storing
  • Repack sensors in original arrangement
  • Re-roll cables correctly — never kink at connectors
  • Check device back in per TF procedure
  • Recharge battery — log charge status
  • Report damage immediately

Acoustic vs. Seismic Sensors

Two fundamentally different sensor types detect different phenomena over different ranges. Most cache devices (Delsar LD3, Delsar Mini) carry both.

Acoustic Sensor — "Microphone"
  • Detects airborne sound — voice, crying, calling
  • Frequency: 200–3,000 Hz
  • Range: 5–25 ft (1.5–7.5 m)
  • Best when victim can vocalize
  • More affected by ambient noise
Seismic Sensor — "Geophone"
  • Detects structure-borne vibration — knocking, movement
  • Frequency: 1–3,000 Hz
  • Range: 50–150 ft (15–45 m)
  • Works even if victim cannot vocalize
  • Placed on structural surfaces
Deploy Seismic First

Seismic sensors (50–150 ft) cover far more area than acoustic sensors (5–25 ft). Deploy seismic first for initial area sweep, then use acoustic for confirmation at close range.

Detection Range Reference

Sensor TypeSignal DetectedFrequencyDetection RangeMax Spacing
AcousticVoice, crying, calling200–3,000 Hz5–25 ft (1.5–7.5 m)10–20 ft
SeismicKnocking, movement, scratching1–3,000 Hz50–150 ft (15–45 m)Max 25 ft; typ. 15 ft
Seismic (steel)Knocking on pipe / beamFull rangeUp to 150+ ftAdjust per noise level
Seismic (soft soil)Knocking, movementAttenuated5–15 ftReduce significantly
FEMA Doctrine

Sensor spacing should not exceed 25 ft (8 m) under any conditions. Typically, 15 ft (5 m) spacing will cover the area well even under difficult circumstances. One sensor may suffice for hasty detection — locating requires at least two.

Victim Signs of Life — Hierarchy

Not all signs of life are equally detectable. This hierarchy — strongest to weakest — should guide your confidence level in a find.

SignalDetectabilitySensor TypeNotes
KnockingSTRONGESTSeismic, 50–150 ft3 deliberate knocks — clearest, most reliable signal.
Voice / CallSTRONGAcoustic, 5–25 ftShouting, crying — requires consciousness and open airway.
Movement / ScratchMODERATESeismic, reduced rangeShifting, scratching — fainter, less rhythmic than knocking.
BreathingWEAKSeismic, very close rangeVery faint, low amplitude — near equipment limit.
Heartbeat vibrationWEAKESTSeismic, extreme limitEdge of equipment capability — rarely usable alone.
Critical Limitation

Listening devices CANNOT detect an unconscious victim who cannot generate a signal. Canine or other technology (UWB radar, thermal) is required to find unconscious or non-responsive victims.

Where Listening Devices Fit

Listening devices are one tool in a layered search strategy. No single tactic is sufficient on its own — the most effective search blends all viable methods.

Search MethodDetects Unconscious?Best StructureSpeed
Physical Void / Call-OutNOAll types — light frame bestFast initial sweep
Canine (K9)YESLight frame, rubble, precastFast, needs rest periods
Electronic ListeningNOConcrete frame, pancake collapseSlower, site-specific
Search Camera / FiberopticYESAny — needs void accessSlow — confirms listening finds
Thermal / IR ImagingYES (heat)Open areas, dark / smokeFast survey — does not penetrate solids
UWB Radar (modern)YES (movement)Through 50cm concrete, ~100 ftFast — detects breathing movement

Advanced Operations

The Intercom Probe
  • Dual function: Can be lowered or inserted into a void to listen AND talk to a trapped victim.
  • Isolation: Isolates high-frequency sounds such as breathing and groaning.
  • Use: Push the talk button and speak into the microphone to communicate directly with the victim.
  • Note: Once connected, sensor scrolling is disabled and all filtering turns off automatically.
T-Cable Junction

Allows running two sensor strings in different directions instead of one sequential line — useful for L-shaped structures or corner searches where a single line cannot cover two search directions.

Stereo Comparison Technique

A Y-splitter lets two operators listen simultaneously on different sensors for faster real-time comparison than switching between channels alone. Particularly useful during locating mode when rapid amplitude comparison is needed.

Source: Firehouse Magazine — "Life Detection Systems," Capt. Tony Tricarico, FDNY Squad 252 / Special Operations Command.

Power Sources & Filtering

Power Source Options

Power SourceTypical RuntimeNotes
6× C-cell batteries~24 hoursStandard field configuration.
AC adapter (laptop-style)Unlimited (while connected)For staging area / extended ops.
12V vehicle battery adapterUnlimited (while connected)Field power from apparatus.

Filter Types

FilterEffectTradeoff
Rumble filterReduces low-frequency noiseReduces detection range.
PWR filterCuts narrow frequency bandDoes NOT reduce detection range.
HISS filterCuts high frequenciesCosts clarity on scratching sounds.
Always Listen Unfiltered First

All listening should begin in unfiltered, wide-band mode before any filter is applied — to avoid missing weak signals like scratching or breathing.

Source: Firehouse Magazine — "Life Detection Systems" / Savox Delsar LD3 Operation and Maintenance Manual.

Knowledge Check & Summary

Key Takeaways

  • Sound travels INTO rubble more easily than OUT — call-out and knocking protocol comes first.
  • Detection confirms a victim exists; locating pinpoints where — treat them as separate phases.
  • Seismic sensors (50–150 ft) cover far more area than acoustic sensors (5–25 ft) — deploy seismic first.
  • Sensor spacing should never exceed 25 ft; 15 ft is the practical standard.
  • Always listen unfiltered first — filtering can hide weak victim signals.
  • No single search method is sufficient alone — combine listening, canine, and visual confirmation.
  • Equipment care matters — cable connector pins are the most fragile, most mission-critical component.

Discussion Questions

  1. What is the difference between detecting and locating a victim?
  2. Why should a victim be asked to knock rather than yell?
  3. What happens if four sensors all show equal amplitude in a block pattern?
  4. Name three sources of human-made interference and how to mitigate each.
  5. Why can't listening devices detect an unconscious victim?
  6. What is the detection range difference between acoustic and seismic sensors?
Section 07

Search Cameras

Borescope and rigid camera systems for visual void inspection and victim confirmation.

Section 07 · Search Cameras

Search Cameras — Overview

Flexible borescope or rigid camera systems inserted through drilled openings, breaches, or natural voids to visually inspect inaccessible void spaces. A search camera can confirm or rule out victim presence where acoustic or seismic detection alone cannot — some systems include two-way audio for direct victim contact.

Protocol Summary

Identify insertion point (existing void or Structures Specialist-approved drill location) → clear debris around the opening → insert slowly, announcing camera position if audio-equipped → systematically rotate view through 360° at each void → document with still or video capture at each position → log results in SARCOP (Victim Confirmed if visual confirmation achieved).

More Detail Coming

This overview carries forward the existing camera protocol from Section 4's Technical Search Methods page. Send additional detail — equipment models, specific field techniques, common failure points — and this page will be expanded to match the depth of the Listening Devices section.

Void Insertion & Placement

Technique reference for inserting a search camera into a void space for victim confirmation.

More Detail Coming

Send the field technique details (insertion angle, void prep, how to handle obstructions, lighting considerations, etc.) and this page will be expanded to match the Sensor Placement pages in the Listening Devices group.

Documentation & Confirmation

Standards for documenting search camera findings and confirming victim status.

Placeholder

No documentation standards added yet. Send how findings should be captured (still image vs. video, file naming/storage, SARCOP logging steps) and how camera confirmation combines with other indicators to establish Victim Confirmed status, and this page will be built out.

Field Reference · Technical Search Equipment · Doc A020053#A

Savox SearchCam 3000

Technical search camera for urban search and rescue. Articulating waterproof camera head, high-output LED illumination, ClearSearch display, and two-way victim audio for pinpointing victim locations and assessing extraction routes.

Camera Head Power Module Video Display Headset Tubes & Cable Kit Contents Compliance

Overview

The SearchCam 3000 is a versatile rescue tool for void search. The camera head articulates 240° and records still photos, audio, and video during the search. The two-way audio connection enables direct communication with trapped victims. Recording media is a standard SDHC card in the video display unit.

Camera Head

Camera head specifications
Camera typeColor (P/N 6010-02-000) or Infrared (P/N 6010-02-002)
Camera sensor1/3" 960H Sony EXview HAD CCD II
Lens6 mm / F2.0
Minimum illumination0.1 lux
Viewing distance in total darkness6 m
Field of view280°
Articulation angle240°
Camera tilt radius11 cm
Safety clutchYes
Two-way audioYes
Head dimensionsø 4.7 × 37 cm
Pistol grip controlsYes

Battery dimensionsø 18–18.6 × 68.6–69.3 mm
Operating timeUp to 3 h nominal at 20 °C
Charge temperature0–40 °C
Field note: Battery runtime is rated at 20 °C. Expect reduced operating time in cold-weather deployments; carry the spare battery set (kit includes 8 × 18650 in the molle pouch).

Section 08

K9 / Canine Search

Canine team integration, roles, and field reconnaissance reference.

Section 08 · K9 / Canine Search

Overview

Canine (K9) teams are limited high-value resources that leverage trained scent detection to maximize Probability of Detection (POD) in structural and debris environments where human or technical search alone is limited. K9 teams are assigned by Search Branch — never self-deployed.

Alert Confirmation Rule

Standard protocol requires all canine alerts to be confirmed by a second, independent K9 team before committing extrication resources. A single alert is logged as Victim Detected. Confirmed dual-alert is escalated to Victim Confirmed with technical follow-up.

Independent Asset Deployment

Because K9 teams are limited, they may deploy as independent assets to rapidly assess priority areas rather than remaining tethered to a single squad. Coordinate deployment timing with Search Branch to maximize area coverage per operational period.

K9 Team Members

K9 Team Types

Live Find Detection (LFD)
SARCOP TRACKLOG: LFD ORANGE

Trained to locate living survivors by detecting exhaled human breath and body scent. Deployed in Hasty, Primary, and Secondary operations. Primary tool for rapid area clearance. Handler reads alert behavior — active alert vs. passive alert vs. interest.

Human Remains Detection (HRD)
SARCOP TRACKLOG: HRD BROWN DASHED

Trained to locate deceased human remains. Deployed during Secondary High operations and recovery phases. HRD alert logged as Human Remains Detected — confirmation requires visual or forensic anthropological confirmation before removal.

Handler–Team Leader Communication Protocol

  1. Pre-Deployment Brief
    Handler briefs Team Leader on assigned area, wind conditions, expected ranging pattern, and estimated time for sector coverage. Handler logs tracklog start in SARCOP.
  2. Active Search
    Handler communicates real-time via radio: sector entry, direction of travel, canine behavior changes. Do not distract or approach working K9 without handler permission.
  3. Alert Declaration
    Handler calls: "[K9 NAME] ALERT — [LOCATION DESCRIPTION] — [ALERT TYPE: ACTIVE/PASSIVE]." Team Leader marks location, logs Victim Detected in SARCOP. Technical search team dispatched.
  4. Confirmation Request
    Search Branch requests second independent K9 team for confirmation. First K9 team holds position or continues search — second team approaches from different direction, independent of first team's information.
  5. Post-Sector Debrief
    Handler reports to Team Leader: areas searched, alert locations, areas with K9 interest but no formal alert, canine condition. All logged in SARCOP before K9 departs sector.

How K9s Search

Scent & Noise Discipline

  • No food, strong scents, or tobacco products within 30m of active K9 search area
  • Non-essential personnel clear the search area — foot traffic disrupts scent cone
  • Minimize vehicle traffic and exhaust in downwind areas ahead of search direction
  • No verbal interaction with K9 by non-handlers during active search
  • All Quiet windows benefit K9 search — coordinate timing with acoustic deployments

K9 Welfare & Rotation

  • Maximum 20–30 minute search rotation in structural environments — equal rest between deployments
  • Hydrate after every rotation — minimum 250 ml water in hot conditions
  • Inspect paws for cuts, debris, and heat injury after each rotation in structural debris
  • Monitor for behavioral changes indicating fatigue: slow response, loss of focus, refusal to work
  • Remove from deployment and notify Safety Officer if heat stress, injury, or behavioral fatigue observed
  • Night operations: reflective gear and light attachment on harness required

MA-TF1 K9 Information and Intel Sheet

K9 specific information and intelligence Notes
Section 09

Victim Location & FEMA Markings

Standard FEMA US&R marking system for victim location, structure survey, and hazard communication.

Section 09 · Victim Location & FEMA Markings

Victim Location Indicators

  • Audible signals: voice, tapping, moaning (acoustic / physical)
  • Visual indicators: visible extremity, clothing, personal effects
  • Canine alert at specific location
  • Acoustic/seismic device signal confirmed
  • Witness or survivor account of known victim location
  • Building occupancy records corroborating probable victim position

Victim Confirmation Methods

Victim location requires corroboration by at least two independent methods before committing a breach or entry team.

Confirmed Alive
Two independent indicators: voice contact + camera visual, or canine alert + acoustic signal at same location.
Probable Victim
One indicator (canine alert, acoustic signal, witness report). Treat as live victim pending confirmation. Immediate technical follow-up required.
Confirmed Deceased
Visual confirmation by EMT or medical authority. Location marked. Documented in SARCOP. Fatality management procedures initiated.

Victim Marking

Mark victim locations clearly to support incoming teams and documentation.

  • Live victim: bright orange flagging at access point with team ID and time
  • Probable victim: yellow flagging at indicator location with source and time
  • Deceased: marked per FEMA US&R protocol — do not disturb without ME or law enforcement
  • All victim marks entered in SARCOP with GPS coordinates and description
MA-TF1

US&R Victim Location Marking — Automated Form

FEMA victim marking system (the “V”) · fills and prints a true-size marking sheet
Directions: Select potential/confirmed and live/deceased state, add a pinpoint arrow if the victim isn't at the mark itself, then Print / Save PDF.
Confirmed: must have seen the victim or had two-way communication.
Confirmed: seen human remains. Two K9 alerts are NOT a confirmation — list them in the notes.
Arrow on
Victim(s) removed
K9 alerts, access, exact void location, anything the next crew needs.

Structure Marking

The FEMA US&R structure marking system uses a standardized box symbol applied to the primary entry point of each structure searched.

FEMA Box Mark

Large X divided into four quadrants. Top: date/time of entry. Left: team ID. Bottom: date/time of exit. Right: hazards found. Inside box: victims found (L = live, D = deceased, with count).

  • Applied in spray paint or lumber crayon on primary entry point
  • Minimum 24-inch symbol
  • Entry slash drawn before entering; second slash (completing X) drawn on exit
  • All four quadrants completed before team departs sector

MA-TF1 US&R Search Marking (Exterior) Auto Form

FEMA search marking system · fills the official sticker exactly as issued · mark near the entry point
Directions: Fill out 1 · ENTERING when you go in, then 2 · EXITING when you come out. Press Print / Save PDF and choose “Save as PDF” as the printer destination.
Live (L)
0
Deceased (D)
0
Additional information

MA-TF1 US&R Search Marking (Interior) Auto Form

FEMA search marking system · fills the official sticker exactly as issued · mark near the entry point (interior)
Directions: Fill out 1 · ENTERING when you go in, then 2 · EXITING when you come out. Press Print / Save PDF and choose “Save as PDF” as the printer destination.
Live (L)
0
Deceased (D)
0
Additional information

Search Assessment Marking

  • Single slash (/): team entered structure — search in progress
  • Full X: team exited — search complete for this operational period
  • Circle around X: structure fully searched, no victims found
  • DO NOT ENTER (DNE): structural hazard prohibits entry

Hazard / Structural Information

  • Hazard symbols placed adjacent to main structure mark — specific NFPA or FEMA hazard indicators
  • Gas: large "G" with status (on/off/controlled)
  • Electric: large "E" with status
  • Asbestos: standard ACM placard
  • HAZMAT: UN placard number if known
  • Structural: written notation at mark (e.g., "LEAN — W WALL UNSTABLE")
MA-TF1

US&R Structure/Hazards Evaluation — Automated Form

FEMA structure/hazards evaluation placard · marks structural stability separately from the search-progress box
Directions: Set the structure condition, add a hazard line and/or arrow to the safest entry if needed, then Print / Save PDF.
HM — hazmat in/adjacent to structure
With the chip on it prints “HM – NATURAL GAS”; chip off prints the hazard text alone.
Arrow on
Printed underneath the marking — falling hazards, shoring needs, safe haven, evac route.
Field version: 2' × 2' box, international orange · empty box = safe · single slash = significant damage · X = not safe · HM = hazmat · arrow → safest entry

Coordinates, Sketches & Map Updates

  • GPS coordinates recorded for all victim locations, structure marks, and hazards
  • Field sketches made for any area not captured in SARCOP / Field Maps
  • Sketch includes: north arrow, scale, structure outline, victim location with descriptor, team ID, date/time
  • All sketches scanned or photographed and uploaded to SARCOP at operational period end
  • Map updates communicated to Documentation Unit in real-time via radio or Field Maps sync
Section 10

SARCOP / Documentation

Common Operating Picture, GIS tools, dashboards, data quality, and tactical documentation procedures.

Section 10 · SARCOP / Documentation

Introduction to SARCOP

SARCOP (Search and Rescue Common Operating Picture) is the GIS-based data collection and mapping system used to track search operations, victim locations, structural hazards, and team assignments across an incident. See Common Operating Picture (COP) for a full explanation of how it works.

  • Every team member with a field data collection role should be familiar with the tools and definitions in this section before deployment
  • Ready to connect a device? Go to Battle Cards for QuickCapture / Field Maps access codes and setup steps

Training & Reference Resources

↗
US&R Field Documentation
responsesystem.org's SARCOP resource hub — field documentation standards and system overview.
responsesystem.org/sarcop
 ↗
SARCOP Training Playlist
ScreenPal video playlist covering SARCOP tools and workflows step by step.
go.screenpal.com/u/FcTw/SARCOPTraining
 ↗
SARCOP Training Sandbox
Live ArcGIS Experience Builder sandbox — practice with SARCOP tools in a non-production environment.
experience.arcgis.com (SARCOP Sandbox)

Common Operating Picture (COP)

The Common Operating Picture consolidates all operational information into a single reference document available to all command staff. SARCOP (Search and Rescue Common Operating Picture) is the GIS-based system that builds and maintains that picture.

What SARCOP Is

SARCOP is built on Esri ArcGIS. Field teams collect data through QuickCapture (fast, button-based entries) and Field Maps (full GIS editing), which syncs into a shared feature service. That live dataset is what populates the Common Operating Picture, the Tactical and Strategic Dashboards, and the printed Battle Card used at briefings.

  • One shared dataset — every team's field entries flow into the same COP, not separate spreadsheets or paper logs
  • Structured by waypoint type, tracklog mission type, search segment, and structural risk marking — all defined in the SARCOP Data Dictionary
  • GIS Coordinator owns the master dataset; field teams contribute via QuickCapture / Field Maps; Command consumes it via the dashboards and Battle Card
  • Data syncs to the server on a schedule (minimum every 30 minutes during active operations) — see Offline, Sync & Data Quality for details
Where to Go Next

To get a device connected and start collecting data, go to Battle Cards for the QuickCapture / Field Maps access codes and setup steps. For field definitions, see Data Dictionary.

Battle Cards

The Battle Card is the printed/digital access point for connecting a device to both QuickCapture and Field Maps — scan the code once in QuickCapture, then reach the correct Field Maps projects from inside it (see Step 4 below).

Field App Access — Training Sandbox

Field Tools Code for the SARCOP Training Sandbox project (opens QuickCapture; Field Maps projects are reached from inside it):

Field App Access — Real World (QuickCapture & Field Maps)

Real World Battle Card — scan to open the live QuickCapture project:

Instructions:

  1. Install the apps
    Install ArcGIS QuickCapture and Field Maps on your mobile device (from your app store) in advance of the steps below.
  2. Sign in
    Once installed, sign in to each app with your Task Force credentials (e.g., motf1_ops).
  3. Scan the QR code
    Scan the QR Code (or click on the link) to open the QuickCapture Project. You can also click "Continue without signing in" to access this public project if you do not have a username.
  4. Access Field Maps projects
    Proceed to the "Additional Apps and Forms" section of the QuickCapture project to access the correct Field Maps projects. See this video or Field Reference Guide for additional details.
  5. Tech Info Specialist — device prep
    Use this "Device Workflow" Job-Aid to prep and maintain your device cache.

Get the Apps

↗
ArcGIS QuickCapture — Google Play
Android install for ArcGIS QuickCapture.
play.google.com (QuickCapture)
 ↗
ArcGIS Field Maps — Google Play
Android install for ArcGIS Field Maps.
play.google.com (Field Maps)
 ↗
ArcGIS QuickCapture — App Store
iPhone install for ArcGIS QuickCapture.
apps.apple.com (QuickCapture)
 ↗
ArcGIS Field Maps — App Store
iPhone install for ArcGIS Field Maps.
apps.apple.com (Field Maps)

GIS Basics

  • All spatial data collected in WGS84 (GPS standard coordinates)
  • Feature layers: structures, segments, victims, hazards, resources
  • Basemap: satellite imagery preferred; topographic as backup
  • GIS Coordinator maintains master dataset; field teams contribute via Field Maps / QuickCapture
  • Data synced to server at minimum every 30 minutes during active operations

Index Manager

Index Manager is the backend catalog tool for managing all SARCOP layers, attachments, and offline data packages.

  • Used by GIS Coordinator to publish map layers for field teams
  • Manages offline map tile packages for areas without cellular coverage
  • Controls feature service permissions and sync settings
  • Archive function preserves incident data at close of operations

SARCOP v10 Data Dictionary

Authoritative definitions for all waypoint types, tracklog mission types, search segments, and structural risk tools used in SARCOP v10, organized by primary category. Last updated May 29, 2026.

↗
SARCOP Data Dictionary (Full PDF)
Complete source document, including logistics waypoints and monitoring point definitions not fully pictured below.
onedrive.live.com (SARCOP Data Dictionary)
Jump to:
Search and Human Interaction Outcomes
Directions / FAQ

No directions or FAQ content added yet for this category. Send how it's used in the field — when to toggle it on/off, what to expect on screen, common mistakes, etc. — and this will be built out to match the Tracklog Mission Types example.

Directions / FAQ

No directions or FAQ content added yet for this category. Send how it's used in the field — when to toggle it on/off, what to expect on screen, common mistakes, etc. — and this will be built out to match the Tracklog Mission Types example.

Search Operations
Directions / FAQ

No directions or FAQ content added yet for this category. Send how it's used in the field — when to toggle it on/off, what to expect on screen, common mistakes, etc. — and this will be built out to match the Tracklog Mission Types example.

Directions / FAQ

This is a toggle, not a one-time log entry — it runs continuously in the background once started.

  • Starting: The moment you begin moving for a recon or search, select the matching mission type (e.g., Primary Search). The device confirms audibly (e.g., "Tracking," "Primary Search") and your track begins recording automatically.
  • While active: The track keeps recording in the background as you walk — no further action needed to keep it running. You can still drop waypoints (victims, hazards, structure marks, etc.) at any time without stopping the track.
  • Stopping: When your recon or search assignment is complete, select the same mission type again to stop and close out the track.
  • Switching: If your task changes mid-track (e.g., recon becomes a primary search), stop the current mission type first, then start the new one — don't run two at once.
Directions / FAQ

Source

SARCOP v10 Data Dictionary — Last updated May 29, 2026. Categories above marked with a placeholder note are covered at a summary level only; refer to the full SARCOP v10 Data Dictionary document for field-by-field definitions.

Offline, Sync & Data Quality

  • Download offline areas before field deployment — do not rely on cellular coverage at collapse sites
  • Sync at every opportunity: when returning to command post, at water/food breaks, at operational period end
  • Inspect incoming data at GIS Coordinator position — flag duplicates, missing fields, and GPS drift
  • GPS accuracy threshold: reject points with HDOP > 5 without manual review
  • Backup: paper logs for all entries in case of device failure

Tactical Dashboards

Real-time operational status view for Search Branch Director and Team Leaders. Displays segment coverage, victim count, team locations, and hazard status.

  • Refreshes automatically from SARCOP feature services every 5 minutes
  • Segment coverage map: color-coded by status
  • Victim summary panel: live / deceased / transported counts
  • Team last-known position and assignment
  • Active hazard list with status

Strategic Dashboard

IC-level overview dashboard aggregating data across all branches and operational periods. Used for resource allocation and mission briefings.

  • Multi-incident / multi-sector aggregation
  • Victim totals across all branches
  • Operational period timeline with coverage progression
  • Resource deployment and availability status
  • Accessible via command post display and secure web link

Documentation & Handoff

  • Operational period documentation completed before relief arrives
  • SARCOP data synced and reviewed by outgoing GIS Coordinator
  • Battle card updated with current status before briefing
  • In-person handoff: outgoing Team Leader brief to incoming — 10 minutes minimum
  • All paper forms scanned and uploaded to SARCOP
  • Incident documentation archived at close of incident per FEMA documentation protocol

Dashboard Operations — Checklists

Working checklists for operating the SARCOP Tactical and Strategic Dashboards — picking the right view, managing the map, filtering, vetting incoming data, and exporting for reports.

Source

MA-TF1 TSS SARCOP Field Operations Checklists — Volume 2 (Field Review Draft 0.9). Verify procedures against the SARCOP Training Playlist (v10) before adoption.

📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Same maps and data, two audiences: STRATEGIC = broad-area progress, key performance indicators, trends, planning and reporting — Task Force Leaders, Incident Support Teams, Incident Management Teams, Plans. TACTICAL = current field observations, search progress, follow-up, rescue status, and continual reassessment of tactical priorities — Search Managers, team-level Plans Managers, Technical Information Specialists, Operations.
  • Dashboards are configurable per incident — appearance, metrics, and functions may be adjusted, so the operational dashboard may differ from training. Both dashboards carry a job-aid link to the dashboard lessons and suggested operational practices.
  • Three strategic indicators beneath the map: Waypoints (mapped field observations in the current view), Confirmed Human Interactions, and Damage Observations — every value reflects the ACTIVE view and filter settings.
  • Confirmed Human Interactions counts CONFIRMED people only: assisted, evacuated, rescued, sheltering in place, confirmed victims, confirmed human remains. Unconfirmed detections (possible victim indication, unconfirmed canine alert, possible human-remains detection) are NOT included — they belong at the tactical level. Never interpret unconfirmed detections as confirmed human interactions.
  • Information tabs beneath the map (select directly or page with the side arrows): person counts by waypoint type, track-log mileage, search outcomes, damage observations — evaluate work completed, search progress, resource activity, geographic coverage, and priorities.
  • Lists on the left (operational areas, divisions, incident information, legend): selecting an item can highlight the map, filter the dashboard, and support comparison across areas. Some lists download tallies and summarized records for ICS forms, situation reports, and the planning process.
  • Tactical follow-up status button filter (upper right): isolate items requiring action, unresolved observations, completed follow-up, and records awaiting review — the fast path to tactical priorities needing reassignment or additional resources.
  • Before briefing or reporting from any dashboard: confirm the correct incident and date filters, know whether you are reading strategic or tactical information, and confirm downloaded totals before they enter official reports or ICS documentation.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Browser zoom vs map zoom: browser zoom (Ctrl/Cmd and + or −) resizes the ENTIRE dashboard — set it first so the map, lists, and indicators are proportioned and legible; map zoom (+/−, lower right) changes only the geographic extent inside the map.
  • Navigate fast: Shift + click-drag draws a zoom box around a segment, neighborhood, or division; a mouse beats a trackpad for precision; the expand icon (upper right) enlarges the map for congested data, detailed review, group briefings, and static displays.
  • Search (map tools, upper right): a complete address or official occupancy name gets the best result — add the municipality if needed. A broad search ("elementary schools") returns several candidates: never assume the first result is correct — confirm the mapped location before navigation or planning use.
  • Legend before interpretation: similar symbols can represent different statuses, priorities, search outcomes, risk categories, or follow-up requirements — open the legend (map tools, or the left list stack) and select features for pop-up detail.
  • Layers: the visibility icon toggles display only (slash = off) — nothing is deleted, nothing changes in SARCOP or for other users. Display what the task needs (assigned segments, tracks, outcomes, hazards, follow-up waypoints); hide what obscures it. Before concluding information is missing, confirm the layer is ON.
  • Scale-dependent and grayed-out layers: some layers draw only at certain zooms — Wide Area Search waypoints may hide at full-incident extent and appear at neighborhood scale. Grayed out = outside the zoom range, still loading, or connectivity: zoom in/out, wait, check the connection, refresh, confirm it is on.
  • Basemap per mission: detailed streets (urban/suburban, addresses, access routes), topographic (remote terrain, wilderness, limited road access), imagery (aerial context — generally PRE-incident conditions), light/dark gray canvas (clutter reduction, low light, weak connectivity — when the operational data itself is the focus). Hover a basemap option for its description.
  • Post-event imagery arrives as an operational LAYER, not a basemap — toggle it, compare it over any basemap, and confirm imagery date, source, and coverage before operational conclusions.
  • Opening workflow: browser zoom → correct dashboard and incident → zoom to the operational area → basemap → layers on/off → legend → search the target → expand for detail.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Dashboard Controls: the arrow on the left edge opens the filter pane — identical in the Tactical and Strategic Dashboards in v10, so one filtering process covers both.
  • v10 change — operational-area filters are ATTRIBUTE-based: incident, branch, and division membership is stamped on the feature record when it is created instead of computed spatially on every filter — faster, more responsive, easier to combine, consistent across dashboard elements.
  • Filters cascade: selecting an incident limits the branch, division, and team lists to what actually has data — impossible combinations disappear. Apply broadest → most specific: Incident → Branch → Division → Date → Team → observation/mission type → Follow-up status.
  • Tactical minimum: Incident + Date + Team — type in the incident search field to shorten the dropdown, and stack filters (e.g., incident + branch + division) to isolate the operational hierarchy.
  • Team missing from the filter list ≠ system error: usually no data exists under the current selections — check the incident and date filters, the team name exactly as entered in QuickCapture/Field Maps, whether data was submitted, and whether it synchronized.
  • Filters ≠ layers: filters decide which records qualify; layer visibility decides whether they draw. A filter can be correct while its layer is off — and a visible layer can be emptied by an active filter. Check both before concluding data is missing.
  • Overfiltered? Empty map, zero indicators, blank lists: remove the most specific filter, widen the date range, confirm data exists for the combination — or reset (arrow, lower-right of the controls pane) and rebuild the sequence.
  • Attribute filters are only as good as data entry: a wrong incident or team name at collection, a point plotted outside the intended area, or unsynced records break the filter picture — coordinate data-quality problems with the TIS, Plans, or GIS specialists.
  • Confirm every active filter before dashboard totals go into a report, a briefing, or the planning process.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Watch recent field observations, team activity, and last known team locations — tracking is a safety and accountability function; a team that stops moving or stops syncing gets a welfare check.
  • Watch for data gaps: segments with no track coverage, teams with no waypoints — gaps mean unsearched ground or sync failures; run both down.
  • Vet before it publishes or gets briefed: Mayfield lesson — an evacuation optimistically recorded as a rescue draws immediate attention at every level.
  • Check pin placement — waypoints must sit on the structures they reference; queue corrections per the ROE.
  • Reconcile duplicates and mislabeled symbols with the field team that collected them before editing.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Work the Strategic Dashboard for analysis: filtering works like the Tactical Dashboard; the tabs beneath the map (configuration-dependent) carry damage observations by search type, track-log mileage, waypoints by type, person counts, waypoints collected by day, and search progress — Plans and IST review incoming data in detail here.
  • Set filters BEFORE exporting — the export reflects the ACTIVE filters: wrong filters produce incomplete or misleading reports. Minimum: Incident + Date + Team; add branch, division, search type, operational period, waypoint type, or follow-up status as the report scope requires. Use the reset control when a COMPLETE dataset is required.
  • Confirm the view first: correct incident, reporting period, and intended team or operational area; review the map, indicators, and charts; verify displayed totals are reasonable; clear unintended filters.
  • Download from the element: select the chart, indicator, list, or table holding the needed information, then its download icon — commonly near the lower portion of the element. Icon not visible? Expand the element, adjust browser zoom, hover over it, confirm the element permits export, or open the dashboard in a separate window.
  • SOURCE vs SUMMARIZED — choose deliberately: SOURCE = the individual records under the element with attributes (each waypoint, date/time, team, squad, observation type, location, comments, person count) — record-level analysis, data-quality review, follow-up assignments, agency handoffs, spreadsheet/GIS work. SUMMARIZED = aggregated totals (waypoints by type, person count by outcome, track-log miles, activity by day, search totals by team) — ICS 209/214, situation reports, briefings, operational-period summaries; no manual counting of records.
  • Before sharing source data: review the file for sensitive information, confirm NO PII is included, share only the necessary fields, protect operationally sensitive information, and follow incident data-sharing procedures.
  • WAYPOINT COUNT ≠ PERSON COUNT: one human-interaction waypoint may represent multiple people — a household, several victims, multiple evacuees (1 Victim Confirmed waypoint can carry a person count of 2). On the ICS 209: the summarized person-count field reports PEOPLE; waypoint totals report observations/locations — confirm which metric the form requires; never substitute one for the other.
  • Validate human-interaction totals before reporting: confirm the active filters and date range, review BOTH waypoint count and person count, check for duplicate or corrected records, question unusual totals — coordinate discrepancies with Plans or the Technical Information Specialists.
  • Every export is a static CSV snapshot (opens in Excel, Google Sheets, ArcGIS, database tools) — it will NOT reflect new, updated, or corrected records. Name consistently: incident, data type, team/area, reporting period, export date-time (e.g., Hurricane_Fiona_Waypoint_Summary_2026-07-06_1800.csv). Open and review the file, confirm totals match the dashboard, then store or distribute per incident procedures — preserve the original export before making changes.
  • Trend exports (waypoints per day, track miles by team, activity by operational period, follow-up status changes, human interactions over time, damage patterns by area) support progress evaluation, gap identification, resource reallocation, and period-to-period comparison.
  • Exports SUPPORT — never substitute for — required incident reporting and validation: the dashboard does not replace unit-level ICS 214 activity documentation, and exports are only as accurate as the field data (wrong team names, duplicate waypoints, unsent offline data, misclassified types) — route significant discrepancies to Plans, TIS, GIS specialists, or search managers.
  • RESL / DOCL: maintain the operational-period documentation — who searched what, when, with what result — from the dashboard record.
  • Confirm every team's data is in hand before demobilization — do not release teams whose track logs are missing (Katrina lesson).
  • Archive the operational period per procedure so the record survives the incident.

TFS Dashboard & Documentation (RESL / DOCL)

Resource tracking through the Team Fact Sheet Dashboard, the RESL/DOCL documentation workflow, and attachments, filing, and SharePoint/OneDrive discipline.

Source

MA-TF1 TSS SARCOP Field Operations Checklists — Volume 2 (Field Review Draft 0.9). Verify procedures against the SARCOP Training Playlist (v10) before adoption.

📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • The resource picture: a centralized ArcGIS view of active SAR resources — status, reported location, personnel and canine counts, resource type, situation, time of report, key contacts, and attached files. A first TFS submission creates the resource record; updates modify it — no duplicate entries.
  • Access: response-system landing page → SARCOP → correct environment (RED training sandbox vs deployment) → More tab → Team Fact Sheet tab. Read the splash screen — it may carry incident-specific instructions; contact the team SARCOP administrator for account or access issues.
  • First filter: Training vs Deployment forms — confirm the environment BEFORE interpreting resource totals or exporting.
  • Work the list: newest submissions and updates on top; search by the most precise resource name. The pop-up delivers status, reported location, situation, report time, leadership contacts and numbers, resource counts, supporting files, and a link to the complete Survey123 form (view-only — or Edit when authorized).
  • Never change a team's information without coordination or a documented operational reason — editing stays with authorized personnel: correcting status or contacts, assisting a team with field access problems, or acting at the submitting team's request.
  • Files: downloading and filing TFS attachments may be assigned to the RESL or DOCL — store them in the incident folder under established naming conventions, version control, and records-management requirements.
  • Pax Count answers "how many personnel are deployed?" — the value reflects ALL active filters (environment, incident, resource type, status): confirm the filters before reporting the total.
  • Resource Summary: resources, personnel by resource, canines, and resource type; expand it for briefings and coordination-center display. Downloads offer SUMMARY data (the aggregated totals shown) or SOURCE data (underlying records for analysis, QC, reconciliation) — choose the level of detail deliberately.
  • Exports are static snapshots shaped by active filters: reset first when a complete dataset is required, review the file for completeness, and time-stamp the filename (e.g., Team_Fact_Sheet_Export_2026-07-06_1500.xlsx) for version control and shift-change continuity.
  • TFS location = a snapshot at report time, NOT a live position. Location sharing (requires license, active sharing, the layer enabled, access, and scale) may show a more recent last-known device position — review the date/time before using either operationally; neither replaces accountability or communications procedures.
  • Behind the dashboard (varies by incident): Power Automate may email designated leaders (IST RESL, NRCC SAR Unit Leader) when a TFS is submitted or updated, and a webhook activity log may back up every submission and update to a spreadsheet — historical entries the current record no longer shows.
  • Download failing while embedded in the SARCOP site? Legacy workaround: overflow menu (upper right) → open the dashboard in a new browser window and export from there — test the embedded function first; it may since be fixed.
  • The dashboard is only as current as the submitted Team Fact Sheets — review report times before basing decisions on them.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.

Source video recorded June 2024. Dashboard controls, access permissions, naming conventions, document-storage procedures, and exact FEMA US&R form numbers should be verified against current program guidance before use.

  • One controlled source, not five separate requests: the TFS is meant to stop Branch, Plans, Logistics, Resource Unit, Documentation Unit, and Comms from each separately asking the same task force for the same contacts, roster, and forms — retrieve it from the dashboard instead of contacting the team directly.
  • Filter before you read anything: deployment type/source (FEMA, State, Local, EMAC), team status (Activated, Alert, Demobilized), transit status (preparing, en route, arrived, operating, returning), update date, and FEMA region — confirm environment, filters, and that duplicates/outdated records are excluded before reporting a PAX count or region-wide total.
  • Retrieve one contact: filter to the deployment → search the team → open details → locate the position (TFL, Plans Team Manager, TIS, Safety Officer, Logistics Team Leader, primary/secondary POC) → verify the phone/email is current.
  • A blank contact field is NOT proof the position doesn't exist: it may mean the position wasn't staffed when the team first submitted — follow up with the team or flag the TFS for an update rather than assuming.
  • Use the Contact List view for a contact export, not the full TFS: it's the leaner, purpose-built list (team, POCs, TFL, LTL) — apply deployment/status filters first, then Export → CSV for an Excel-based list.
  • Clean the export before distributing it: strip Object ID, Global ID, and other internal database fields; keep team name, resource type, primary/secondary POC with phone and email, TFL, Plans Team Manager, LTL, and other required positions.
  • Format for actual use: convert to an Excel table, confirm the header row, apply filters, sort by team name, adjust column widths, freeze the header — this is what gets used on a coordination call, not the raw export.
  • Name it so anyone can identify it cold: Contact_List_YYYYMMDD_HHMM_CreatorRole (e.g., Contact_List_20260706_1910_White_RESL.xlsx) — follow the current IST naming convention rather than this example alone.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Verify before you file, every time: correct form, correct team, correct incident, correct date, legible, complete, signed/approved when required, and free of prohibited information (no PII).
  • Common uploaded forms (verify exact numbers against current doctrine): US&R 1801, 1804, 1815; ICS 205A/205T; 212-series — and other deployment-required forms.
  • Rename on download, not before: FormNumber_YYYYMMDD_TeamName (e.g., 1801_20260706_MA-TF1.pdf) — add a time when multiple versions could land the same day.
  • A corrected version arrives — don't overwrite: preserve the earlier version per documentation policy, name each version clearly, record the update time, and identify which one is current — overwriting destroys the audit trail.
  • Incident and task-force folders follow a naming convention too: incident folder example — 01_Hurricane_Alberta_0624_White_IST; task force folders tagged with resource type or MRP (MA-TF1 – MRP, MD-TF1 – Type 1) so capability is visible at a glance. Keep the full template folder set for a large event; create folders as teams arrive for a smaller one.
  • Sync SharePoint to your desktop for drag-and-drop filing, but VERIFY it before trusting it: Sync → approve the OneDrive prompt → confirm the incident folders appear in File Explorer/Finder — then upload a test document and confirm it shows up BOTH locally and back in the SharePoint library before relying on it.
  • Permissions split by role: Plans, Resource, and Documentation personnel generally need EDIT access (create folders, upload, rename, reorganize, fix filing errors); Logistics generally gets VIEW/DOWNLOAD only — they retrieve forms without renaming or moving shared files. Route permission problems to the SARCOP/IST administrator.
  • Use the Activity Log to find the LATEST submission, not just A submission: filter by team, sort by date/time, newest first — an "Edit Data" entry is a later, likely more accurate update than the original "Add Data" record; open the log in desktop Excel for heavier filtering on a long incident.
  • Missing form? Don't fire off repeated one-off requests to the same team: check for an unexpected filename first, review attachments, the synced folder, and the activity log — then route a single coordinated request through Plans/Logistics.
Before You Distribute a Contact List or File a Form
  • Team, deployment, status, contacts, and personnel count are current — not stale.
  • Form, team, incident, and date match; document is legible, complete, and correctly named and foldered.
  • OneDrive/SharePoint sync confirmed — the file is visible in the shared copy, not just locally.
  • Duplicate versions are controlled and the current version is clearly identified.
  • Permissions are correct for who's receiving it, and no PII or sensitive information is included.

Intel Manager — Orient, Filter & Edit

Intel Manager v10 is the primary browser-based SARCOP application for creating, editing, managing, and analyzing operational data. This page covers orientation, filtering, editing and creating features, and supplemental data. Segment tools, map products, and admin workflows continue on Intel Manager — Segments, Products & Admin.

Source

MA-TF1 TSS SARCOP Field Operations Checklists — Volume 2 (Field Review Draft 0.9). Verify procedures against the SARCOP Training Playlist (v10) before adoption.

📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Intel Manager v10 is the primary SARCOP application for creating, editing, managing, and analyzing operational data — Intel Manager 2.0 became its foundation, so earlier workflows look familiar, but tools, widgets, and configurations may have changed.
  • The map opens to the web map's SAVED extent — possibly another incident, an exercise, or the author's default — NOT necessarily your incident or location: confirm the selected incident before reviewing or editing anything.
  • Layout: central map; "I Want To" Quick Access panel (left) shortcuts the common widgets — Legend, Field Edit, Filters, Layers, Basemap Gallery, Print; widgets open in the right pane (X closes it and enlarges the map); the collapse arrow retracts the left panel for a near-full-screen map; additional widgets live under the three-dot menu (upper right).
  • Select Incident (header) does two things: moves the map to the incident AND filters or hides other incidents' data — the fast way to move between active operations. Before editing or analyzing, verify incident name, geographic location, training vs deployment environment, operational dates, and active filters — the wrong incident means working records in the wrong operational context.
  • Reset Map (near the incident selector) resets the application state and returns the SAVED default extent — it does not necessarily return you to your selected incident: reconfirm extent and incident after every reset.
  • Navigate: +/− or scroll to zoom; Shift + click-drag draws a zoom box (division, segment, neighborhood, worksite); Home = the saved default extent (not the incident, your location, or the previous view); previous/next extent arrows work like back/forward; the bullseye moves to your current location (browser permission required; accuracy varies); the north arrow resets rotation — keep north-up when comparing printed maps, radio reports, and USNG references.
  • Search (upper right) accepts addresses, occupancy names, and USNG: use the complete address (number, street, municipality, state); occupancy results are biased toward the CURRENT extent — navigate to the correct jurisdiction first, and never assume the first result is right; USNG search takes coordinates passed by radio, field report, or assignment — confirm grid zone, precision, format, and incident area.
  • Measure (below search): distance, area, and perimeter. Lines are straight-line estimates — no terrain, debris, flooding, closures, or elevation. Polygons: click the boundary, read area and perimeter in the results panel — segment sizing and preliminary planning. Estimates only: never a substitute for formal GIS analysis when precision matters.
  • Live USNG display (lower left) tracks the cursor. The crosshair tool + a map click pins a coordinate that pauses the display and copies to the clipboard — paste into widgets, reports, assignments, and ICS documentation. Confirm the selected point, the required precision, and the incident area before distributing.
  • Startup workflow: environment → incident → extent → filters → layers → legend → basemap → operational area → USNG display and orientation → open only the widgets the task needs.
  • Wrong data or empty map? Re-check Select Incident, active filters, layer visibility, zoom level, and connectivity — reset and rebuild if the state is unclear.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Filters change YOUR current view only: nothing is deleted, edited, or hidden for other users — QuickCapture, Field Maps, dashboards, and the underlying records are untouched.
  • Open the widget from the "I Want To" panel or the funnel icon (upper right). Expand a category's arrow, search or select the value, then TURN THE TOGGLE ON — selecting a value alone does not activate the filter.
  • Team filter: expand Team Name, search the list, select, toggle on — review your team's observations, run quality control, find incomplete work, review search coverage, prepare briefings and end-of-shift debriefs, locate records needing correction.
  • Follow-up status filter takes MULTIPLE values (e.g., Needs Follow-Up + Assigned — records matching either display, still respecting other active filters) — isolates unresolved and assigned work: victim-related observations, planning points, worksites awaiting action.
  • Select the incident FIRST to shrink filter lists: the Team Name list then shows only teams with data for that incident — faster selection, no similarly named team from another incident. A team missing from a list usually means no data submitted under the current selections, not a system error.
  • Stack filters — all active criteria must match: incident + team + follow-up status answers "what assigned work remains incomplete?", "which observations from this incident need follow-up?", "what should the debrief cover?", "what needs reassignment or correction?"
  • Toggle OFF ≠ reset: toggling off disables the filter but RETAINS its selections — compare filtered vs unfiltered views, then reactivate without rebuilding a complex query. Resetting removes the selections. Reset All Filters = the circular arrow at the bottom of the pane: use it when starting a new analysis, switching incidents or teams, or when the map goes unexpectedly empty.
  • Reset Map ≠ Reset All Filters: Reset Map returns the saved extent and application state and does NOT clear filters; Reset All Filters clears selections and restores the unfiltered view. Map navigation never disables a filter — only its toggle or a reset does.
  • Filters ≠ layers (same doctrine as the dashboards): filters decide which records qualify; layer visibility decides whether they draw. Expected records missing? Confirm the selections, the toggle, the layer, the zoom level, and the incident — in that order.
  • Apply broadest → most specific (incident → team → date/operational period → observation or mission type → follow-up/status) — broader filters shrink the options inside the specific ones. Overfiltered? Turn off the most specific filter, widen the dates, or Reset All and rebuild.
  • ESCALATEFilter results are only as good as the records: wrong team names, missing incident attributes, inconsistent Squad IDs, wrong-project submissions, unsynced field data, stale follow-up status. Filtering FINDS inconsistencies; it never fixes them — correct through Field Edit or another approved workflow, and coordinate data-quality issues with the TIS or GIS specialist.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Field Edit updates existing features — waypoint attributes, follow-up status, comments, segment assignment and status, polygon geometry, point locations — to correct, complete, or update records WITHOUT changing the original meaning of the field observation.
  • Open and select: "I Want To" → Create or Edit a Waypoint, Logistics Point, or Segment → Field Edit (or the toolbar icon) → Edit Features selection tool → click the point, line, or polygon. Multiple features can stack at one location — confirm feature type, attributes, and record details before touching anything.
  • Waypoint forms carry the QuickCapture record (team, Squad ID, operator, type, date/time, follow-up, comments); some fields arrive defaulted, and some are READ-ONLY (original type, collection time, system identifiers) — protected fields preserve the original observation and its history.
  • Close out a waypoint: add a concise operational comment ("No occupants located. Reverse 911 confirmed homeowner safely self-evacuated."), change follow-up from Needs Follow-Up to Complete / No Follow-Up Needed, select Update — the red follow-up halo drops; the original record is never deleted.
  • Confirm every update: with connectivity, changes appear within seconds — reopen the pop-up and verify the comment, status, symbology, location, incident, and feature type. Weak signal: wait and refresh; do NOT resubmit unless the first edit is confirmed failed.
  • Segments — same workflow, polygon form: completion status, assigned task force/team/squad, search type, work-area description, special instructions, team comments, debrief information, metrics. Activate an assignment: Inactive → Active, select the task force, add access, hazards, and instructions — review everything before Update.
  • Search metrics auto-calculate: structures, acreage, structures per acre, road mileage, and estimated search duration (squads × search type × structures per squad-hour). VERIFY whether the entered rate applies per squad or to the combined resource before the estimate goes operational. Suggested rates are planning assumptions, not performance standards — damage, access, hazards, weather, documentation, and rescue activity change actuals.
  • Polygon geometry: orange vertices drag to adjust a boundary. Before changing one: confirm the change is authorized, creates no unintended gaps, no improper overlap with another assignment, stays tactically manageable, and assigned teams are informed — geometry changes ripple into metrics, structure counts, mileage, assignments, and completion reporting.
  • Move a mislocated point (e.g., a damage observation sitting in the roadway): select it in Field Edit, wait for the four-direction arrow, drag it onto the OBSERVED feature — never on assumption; use imagery, addresses, building footprints, field notes, photographs, team confirmation, local knowledge (same pin doctrine as Field Maps).
  • Know your lane: routine users correct their own recent observations, follow-up status, comments, and point locations. Coordinate through the TIS, Plans Team Manager, Search Manager, GIS, or Operations leadership before reassigning a segment, changing boundaries, modifying another team's record, deleting or recycling data, or changing completed search status.
  • Preserve the original record: clarify with comments, keep the operational history, never replace valid information without explanation, and never reinterpret another team's observation without coordination.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Intel Manager creates PLANNED features: medical posts, casualty collection points, drop points, staging areas, missing-person initial planning points, search segments. QuickCapture remains the tool for rapid field observations (outcomes, damage, hazards, human interactions, tracks) — use the application the incident workflow designates.
  • Before creating anything: correct incident AND environment; the feature does not already exist (search the map, check layers and filters — duplicates create conflicting assignments, bad counts, dashboard errors, duplicate follow-up work); location accurate; correct template; operationally authorized; required information in hand.
  • Create a point: select the template (e.g., Medical) → click the actual location → the smart form opens (fields differ by feature type). Label: brief, unique, operationally recognizable, per incident convention ("Pearl River Medical"). Comments: services, hours, access, limitations ("ALS services available"). Enter the creator's name; keep default workflow fields unless procedures direct otherwise → review location, type, label, and required fields → Create — the feature then appears across applicable SARCOP maps and applications.
  • Missing Person Initial Planning Point = the shared starting location for a search operation (last known point, point last seen, reported water-entry location). Comments document the BASIS: witness information, direction of travel, time last seen, conditions — NO PII unless specifically authorized by system, workflow, and incident policy. Mark Needs Follow-Up while operations remain; set priority by life-safety, time sensitivity, and report reliability; enter the team when known — leave optional fields blank rather than guessing. Person count = PEOPLE represented, not the number of records.
  • Search segments — draw the polygon: select the Search Segments template and status (Not Active for a pre-planned segment) → click each corner of the boundary → DOUBLE-CLICK the final vertex to close the shape and open the form.
  • Boundaries teams can recognize: roads, waterways, rail lines, property/neighborhood/administrative lines, terrain features, existing segment borders — avoid needlessly complex shapes. Segments must be clearly identifiable, tactically manageable, accessible to the assigned resource, matched to the search method, and distinct from adjacent assignments.
  • Snapping makes clean shared borders: enable snapping in the Field Edit settings AND select Search Segments as the snapping layer (snapping may default on, but the layer must still be chosen) — vertices align to existing boundaries, eliminating gaps, overlaps, slivers, and ambiguous assignment lines. After drawing: zoom in and inspect shared edges, stray vertices, unintended overlap, unassigned gaps.
  • Segment form: unique radio-friendly name per incident convention; mission type (hasty / primary / secondary / reconnaissance — it feeds the search-rate assumptions); task force and Squad ID when known (or the unassigned status per procedure); access route, entry point, hazards, priorities, communications, staging/reporting location. Auto-calculated metrics (structures, acreage, structures per acre, road miles, estimated duration) support resizing and resourcing before the segment is issued.
  • Required fields carry an asterisk: a blocked Create points to the missing field — complete it and select Create again (same smart-form behavior as RST).
  • After Create: select the feature, review the pop-up, attributes, geometry, label, and symbology; confirm the correct incident; check other SARCOP apps when it matters; notify personnel whenever the new feature affects assignments or operations.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Temporarily overlay supplemental layers (hazard models, weather, flood, infrastructure, population, boundaries, reference data) against SARCOP field data — tracks, damage observations, waypoints, building footprints, segments — for situational awareness, impact analysis, hazard comparison, and next-period planning.
  • SESSION-SPECIFIC — yours only: added data appears only in YOUR current Intel Manager session — never in QuickCapture, Field Maps, dashboards, or anyone else's display, and it does not survive a restart. A layer everyone needs goes through the formal SARCOP GIS review workflow.
  • Select the incident FIRST (moves the map, limits displayed data, cuts clutter, improves responsiveness) — then the three-dot More Widgets menu → Add Data.
  • Sources: search the CURATED SARCOP collection (hazard, weather, flood, infrastructure, population, and reference layers pre-selected for SAR) before wading through public ArcGIS Online; also My Organization / My Content / My Groups (agency and incident content — account and sharing permissions apply); a service or layer URL (must point at the layer or service itself, not a webpage); or file upload — shapefile, CSV, KML, GeoJSON, GPX.
  • Vet public layers before operational use: owner, source, date, geographic coverage, update frequency, methodology, limitations — appearing in ArcGIS Online does NOT make a layer current or authoritative.
