  • Upload checks: valid geometry and coordinates, correct area, no sensitive or unauthorized content, size appropriate for a web application (large or complex files load slowly, degrade performance, or fail); package all required shapefile components together.
  • Manage the added layer: rename it for THIS session by hazard/scenario/date ("Category 3 SLOSH Inundation" — the source item is unchanged); Zoom To confirms coverage against the incident; toggle visibility, read the legend, open item details, adjust transparency; the trash can removes it from your session without deleting the source.
  • Legend and item details BEFORE interpretation: units, categories, color meaning, and model scenario — depth vs elevation vs probability; owner, date, terms of use, and limitations decide whether the layer is suitable for operational analysis.
  • Compare, don't obscure: raise transparency until observations, footprints, tracks, and roads read through the model; switch basemaps for context (wetlands, terrain, access) — basemap is context: confirm date and source. Worked example: Category 3 SLOSH surge over damage observations and Wide Area Search data exposes severe-impact areas with limited reporting, search gaps, flooded access routes, and next-operational-period priorities.
  • Models are scenarios, not ground truth: no actual storm track, protective features, drainage, or real-time conditions — observed impacts may be less severe, more severe, or distributed differently. A model-vs-field mismatch is an analytical FINDING, not proof either dataset is wrong. Added data informs planning; it never determines assignments without validation.
  • Keep the map lean: add only the layers that answer the current question — every extra dataset costs clarity and load time; remove layers when done. Needed again after a restart? Re-add it, keep the source URL, and coordinate with GIS if it should become permanent.

Intel Manager — Segments, Products & Admin

Advanced Intel Manager workflows: copying reference layers into operational features, batch editing, printing map products, Admin Editor, USNG segment grid tools with the Cut tool, and the Advanced Editor 2.0 beta. Orientation, filtering, and Field Edit basics live on Intel Manager — Orient, Filter & Edit.

Source

MA-TF1 TSS SARCOP Field Operations Checklists — Volume 2 (Field Review Draft 0.9). Verify procedures against the SARCOP Training Playlist (v10) before adoption.

📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Copy geometry and selected attributes from existing layers into SARCOP features: search segments from census or flood-hazard boundaries, targeted-search waypoints from critical infrastructure, planning points from local datasets — less manual drawing, more consistent boundaries. Copy a reference feature only when it genuinely suits the intended operational feature.
  • Census block groups as segment starting points: defined boundaries, population and housing information, full jurisdiction coverage without intentional gaps — but a block group is NOT automatically a search segment: evaluate structures, density, acreage, road mileage, damage, access, terrain, search method, available squads, and expected completion time — divide large or complex ones.
  • Prep the display first — ONLY the source and destination layers visible: fewer selection errors, a shorter template list, a cleaner map. A keyboard modifier + a layer's visibility checkbox can toggle many layers at once — confirm the shortcut for your OS and browser.
  • Workflow: open the editing widget (the copy workflow may appear under a Smart Editor label — confirm in the deployed app) → destination template (e.g., Search Segment) → copy-method menu near the bottom → Copy by Lasso → draw around the source features → in the source list, UNCHECK every layer except the intended source.
  • Copy Features = one destination record per source polygon. Then turn the source layer off and review: every intended feature copied, valid polygons, boundaries align with the source, no duplicates, correct destination layer.
  • Copied geometry ≠ finished segment: complete the name, status, mission type, task force, squad, operational period, priority, work-area description, and instructions — and review the auto-calculated metrics (structures, acreage, structures per acre, mileage, estimated duration).
  • Create One Multipart Feature = several polygons, ONE record — only when the areas function as one assignment: same team and mission type, one operational period, logically connected. Avoid it when it would confuse field personnel, need unrelated access routes, cross divisions or branches, or muddy progress reporting — separate segments are often clearer.
  • Points copy too — worked example: the AHJ reports possibly unstable electrical substations → Add Data pulls a HIFLD substation layer (vet source, date, completeness, positional accuracy, sharing restrictions, and whether the local jurisdiction has newer data) → copy the applicable points into Waypoints as Targeted Search → each substation becomes an assignable, prioritizable, trackable waypoint.
  • Batch-set the new waypoints to Needs Follow-Up with the Batch Attribute Editor — the inspections have not happened yet.
  • Copying never changes the source feature — but review every transferred attribute: source and destination fields may not mean the same thing, values may be outdated, required destination fields may sit blank, and operational names must follow incident conventions.
  • Duplicates: search existing records, review the destination layer and active filters, confirm another planner has not already run the workflow, coordinate with Plans/GIS — duplicates create conflicting assignments, inflated dashboard counts, duplicate follow-up, unclear completion status.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Update the same attributes across many features at once: assign follow-up, apply approval status, prepare the next operational period, clear training records. Power-tool warning: a bad selection changes MANY records in one save — filter, select, and review before touching Save.
  • Separate editors per layer — Waypoints, Track Logs, Search Segments, Event Points, RST/hazard assessments — and the batch-editable fields differ by layer, feature type, permissions, and configuration; only configured fields appear.
  • FILTER FIRST — minimum incident + task force/team (+ date/operational period as needed): the editor respects the active filtered view, or build one from the pencil icon inside the tool. Never rely on geographic proximity alone — several teams and operational periods overlap the same ground.
  • Select with the Freehand tool: draw around the intended waypoints — best for clusters, irregular areas, and dodging unrelated points. Before editing: expected feature count, all records belong to the intended team, no strangers in the selection, extent and filters correct — zoom in and work in manageable groups.
  • Approval ≠ follow-up: Approved = reviewed, vetted, accepted as valid data; Needs Follow-Up = operational action still required. A record can correctly be BOTH — approval never means the need is resolved.
  • Blank-field risk at Save: review the selected count and EVERY field being changed — some tools overwrite valid values with blanks; confirm whether blank or unchanged fields are ignored or applied, and test on a few training records when uncertain.
  • Confirm after saving: spot-check pop-ups, statuses, halos and symbols, and the applicable dashboard — changes ripple across every SARCOP application using the layer (subject to connectivity and refresh timing).
  • SANDBOX ONLYClearing training data — sandbox only: filter by exercise, team, date, and training area; select carefully; set the configured removal field (Delete This / Send to Recycle Bin) to Yes; Save. Records leave the standard display, but this is a RECYCLE-BIN workflow, not permanent deletion — follow SARCOP data-retention and recovery procedures.
  • NEVER batch-remove real-world incident data without specific authorization — operational records serve incident documentation, legal review, after-action analysis, and retention requirements; coordinate through the TIS, Plans Team Manager, GIS, or Documentation Unit. Cleared something by accident? STOP editing, record exactly what was selected and changed, and notify the TIS/GIS — the recycle-bin workflow, activity log, or backup may restore it.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Configure the map before printing. Confirm the correct incident, operational area, filters, visible layers, basemap, and current information — hidden or filtered data will not print. Open the Print Widget through "I Want To" → Print or the printer icon.
  • Show the print area. Select Show Print Area to preview the exact extent for the chosen template. Pan and zoom until the full assignment area, access routes, relevant segments, landmarks, and surrounding context are inside the frame.
  • Select the appropriate template. 8.5×11 landscape for individual assignments and handouts, 11×17 for larger field maps, 24×36 for command-post displays — map-only and screenshot options may also be available. Confirm current options in the deployed system.
  • Replace the default title "SARCOP." Use [Incident] – [Operational Area] – [Map Purpose] (e.g., "TC Fiona – Lakeshore, Mississippi – Operations"). Date and time populate automatically.
  • Review Advanced Settings. Confirm the print extent, author, legend, north arrow, and scale bar. Use a unit or organization as the author, include the legend when symbols may be unfamiliar, and select mission-appropriate scale units.
  • Set the resolution. 300 DPI for final operational maps, lower for rapid electronic sharing. The scale bar is only accurate at the intended print size — resizing the page can invalidate scale interpretation.
  • Generate and review the PDF. Select Print, then open the finished product from the Results tab. Verify the area, title, date/time, north arrow, scale bar, layers, labels, legend, and author information.
  • Save and distribute the product. Print it, place it in the incident folder, attach it to an assignment, or load a georeferenced PDF into a compatible offline mapping app. Use a standardized, time-stamped filename.
  • Verify georeferencing before navigation. Confirm the app recognizes the georeferenced PDF, compare the displayed position with a known point, test the workflow offline, and follow device/data-security procedures. Screenshot outputs are not georeferenced.
  • Control map versions. Every print is a static snapshot. Keep the creation date/time visible, use version-controlled filenames, and remove outdated copies from circulation.
  • GIS specialists: use the current SARCOP ArcGIS Pro template for advanced layouts, labeling, legends, inset maps, and grids. Confirm the current version and resource location.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Admin Editor is for designated data managers (Plans Team Managers, TIS, IST Plans, GIS staff) and does exactly two jobs: create and manage operational-area boundaries, and restore records marked for removal. It is NOT for routine editing of waypoints, segments, or event records.
  • Access: the application dropdown beside Intel Manager → Admin Editor (account permissions apply). Read the About section first — it carries the working instructions for both workflows.
  • The Layers list splits in two: TOP = editable operational areas (Incident Areas, Branches, Divisions) plus non-editable reference views of operational data (context only); BOTTOM = records marked for removal.
  • Hierarchy is doctrine: every division inside its branch, every branch inside its incident area — SARCOP filtering, dashboards, summaries, assignment management, and reporting all key off these attributes; a broken hierarchy breaks the filters.
  • Draw operational areas like search segments: select the layer, click the vertices, double-click to close, complete ALL required attributes (names, parent area, status, period, creator, comments), Save — the boundary then serves every applicable SARCOP application.
  • Boundary standards: clear, IAP-consistent, easy to communicate, on recognizable features (roads, rivers, rail, jurisdictional lines, coastline) — no unintended gaps, no unnecessary overlaps, no orphan divisions or branches.
  • Or copy an existing boundary (county, municipality, census area, evacuation zone) via Add Data + the copy-features workflow — e.g., county → Division. Select ONLY the intended source polygon and destination layer: a wrong copy corrupts dashboard filtering, incident navigation, attribution, and reporting. QC after: source layer off, check name/parent/gaps/overlaps, test the area in filters, notify personnel of significant boundary changes.
  • Removal is recoverable, not deletion: the Delete This workflow (Yes — Editing Mistake / Yes — No Longer Needed) HIDES records from standard maps while the data remains underneath — supporting mistake recovery, documentation, auditability, and records-management requirements.
  • Restore a hidden record: open the removed-record layer, locate and CONFIRM the record, set Delete This = No, document the reason in Edit Comments ("Restored after confirming the waypoint was valid and had been marked as an editing mistake in error."), Save — it reappears with its original attributes intact; allow connected apps to refresh.
  • The restoration view edits ONLY Delete This + Edit Comments — everything else is read-only by design; make further authorized corrections afterward through Intel Manager Field Edit. Never recreate a hidden record: restoring preserves the identifier, collection time, and history, and avoids duplicates.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.

Source video refers to Intel Manager 1.0 and "Smart Editor." Confirm current widget names (may appear as Field Edit or another editing interface) against the deployed Intel Manager interface before use.

  • Build the temporary grid with GRG: More Widgets → Gridded Reference Graphic → Define a Grid from an Area → By Reference System → confirm USNG (or MGRS) — verify the system and cell size even if the widget defaults to one.
  • Size the cell to the workload, not just to precision: a segment should run roughly one-half to one full operational period for the assigned resource — weigh search type, squad count, structure density, roads, terrain, damage, access, hazards, and documentation load before locking in a cell size.
  • Leave "Clip Grid to GRG Area" UNCHECKED: clipping creates partial cells and slivers along the drawn extent that are hard to assign and inconsistent with standard USNG cells — draw the extent wide enough that clipping isn't needed.
  • Grid crosses a UTM/grid zone boundary: build the GRG separately for each zone, verify labels carefully, and loop in a GIS specialist — avoid duplicate or overlapping cells.
  • A GRG is temporary and personal — visible only on the creating user's screen, not shared, not assignable, and not status-tracked until its cells are copied into Search Segments.
  • Copy into Search Segments to make it real: open the feature-editing tool → target the Search Segment destination → Copy by Lasso → select ONLY the GRG cell layer (uncheck the extent/boundary layer) before copying — cancel and retry if the wrong cells are captured.
  • Field-match the grid label to Segment Name BEFORE Create Features: Apply Field Matching → match Grid / Grid Label to the Segment Name field — skip this and the new segments are created unnamed and unusable.
  • Complete every operational attribute after creation: name, status, mission type, priority, TF/squad assignment, operational period, work-area description, special instructions — then review calculated metrics (structures, acreage, road mileage, estimated search duration) to confirm the cell is tactically manageable.
  • USNG is a reference frame, not tactical doctrine: check existing AHJ boundaries first (fire districts, police sectors, evacuation zones) — they're often more useful because responders already know them; subdivide, combine, or realign a raw grid cell against roads, waterways, access, and hazards before it goes to a team.
  • Grid Overlay ≠ Search Segments: use it only when personnel need a visual USNG reference on the map — it carries no assignment, status-tracking, or reporting capability.

Cut Tool — Splitting an Existing Segment

  • Cut divides an editable polygon into two or more pieces: use it when a segment is oversized, needs to follow a road or river, or contains a large area that doesn't need the same assignment. Works only on editable layers (Search Segments, Divisions, Branches, Incident Areas) — a non-editable reference layer (county, census tract, hazard model) must first be copied into an editable layer.
  • Turn off every layer except the target before cutting: overlapping polygons risk selecting or cutting the wrong feature, or editing a reference layer instead of the operational one.
  • Access: overflow menu → Advanced Edits → scissors icon (icon position can shift, since the overflow menu remembers the most recently used tool).
  • Draw the cut line fully outside-to-outside: start outside the polygon, cross completely through it, end outside — a line that starts or ends inside will not produce a valid cut. Align it to a recognizable feature (street, river, rail line, property boundary) so the split is easy to brief and navigate.
  • Rename both halves immediately: unique names only (e.g., Segment 12A / 12B, North / South) — never leave duplicate segment names.
  • Review inherited attributes on both halves: don't assume TF/squad assignment, mission type, status, priority, and comments still apply correctly to each new piece.
  • Re-check search metrics after the cut: structures, acreage, structures/acre, road mileage, and estimated duration recalculate with the new geometry — confirm each half is still a manageable single assignment.
  • Hide, don't delete, an unneeded resulting polygon: Delete This = Yes (appropriate reason) + comment + Save — hides it from standard maps while the record survives underneath for restoration through Admin Editor.
  • Check the new boundary before calling it done: look for slivers, gaps, and overlaps — a cut placed too close to an edge creates map clutter, skews metrics, and can cause dashboard or filtering confusion.
  • Notify affected personnel: a cut to an active assignment boundary changes what a team is responsible for — coordinate with the Search Manager, Plans Team Manager, TIS, and GIS specialist.
  • ESCALATEEscalate to a GIS specialist for high-volume, complex, or persistent-sliver cuts — ArcGIS Pro handles these more efficiently than the web editor.

Segment Closeout

  • Vet incoming intel before it becomes an assignment — a targeted-search point sends a team; make it worth sending them.
  • EVERY OP PERIODPublish updates on the operational-period rhythm so field, dashboard, and plans all see the same picture.
  • Archive segment status, assignments, and intel layers at each period close — the record supports the AHJ after demobilization.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
Beta Preview

The application, interface, tools, calculations, licensing requirements, and workflows may change before production release. Do NOT use beta functionality on real-world operational data unless specifically authorized — work in the sandbox.

  • BETAAccess requires: membership in the designated preview group, the correct ArcGIS license assigned to the account, permission to open Advanced Editor, and editing rights on the applicable SARCOP layers — contact the SARCOP administrator if the app isn't visible, sign-in fails, editing tools are unavailable, or required layers don't appear.
  • Prepare the map before selecting anything: correct incident, zoom to the operational area, turn off layers not needed for the edit, leave only the relevant reference and editable layers visible — overlapping layers are how a boundary gets selected instead of a segment.
  • Confirm the selected feature count before applying any tool: Split generally requires exactly ONE selected polygon; Merge requires MULTIPLE; attribute/batch editing can apply to one or several — the tool available depends on what's selected.
  • Batch-edit shared attributes across selected segments (status, mission type, assigned task force, squad count, special instructions) — open Attributes, confirm the smart form shows "Multiple Features," then apply.
  • Never overwrite fields that should stay unique in a batch edit: leave Segment Name alone when each polygon has its own valid identifier; enter special instructions only when the same text truly applies to every selected segment — squad assignment can be left blank in batch if squads will be assigned per-segment later.
  • Calculated search metrics (structures, acreage, density, road mileage, estimated duration) SHOULD differ segment to segment: they're computed from each polygon's geometry, building footprints, and roads — review every value for reasonableness rather than assuming the calculation is correct.
  • Search-planning inputs (squads, mission type, production rate) come from the Search Team Manager: these are planning assumptions, not fixed performance standards (demonstration used ~6 mph for a hasty search and ~30 structures/squad-hour for a primary search — illustrative starting points only) — actual production varies with debris, road conditions, visibility, population, damage, stops for assistance, documentation, and hazards.
  • Estimated duration exceeds an operational period? Adjust the number of squads, the production rate, or the segment itself (e.g., split it) — do NOT manipulate the planning assumptions just to force the estimate to fit; the plan has to be realistic, not convenient.
  • Before saving any batch edit: recheck the selected feature count, confirm the common attributes, confirm unique names weren't overwritten, review the planning assumptions, and confirm the task-force assignment applies to every selected segment.

Paste Special — Copying One Source Feature

  • Copy ONE feature at a time: clear any previous selection, turn on only the intended reference layer, select a single source polygon → Copy → Paste Special → destination = Search Segments → complete the smart form.
  • Because only one polygon is copied, the form shows calculated metrics for that specific feature: complete segment name, mission type, status, task force, squad count, search-rate assumptions, and special instructions before selecting Create.
  • Multi-feature copy is NOT yet available inside 2.0: for now, copy several source features through the older Advanced Editor 1.0 workflow, then open 2.0 to batch-edit the resulting segments — expect this to change as the beta matures; don't treat the current limitation as permanent.

Merge & Split

  • Merge combines 2+ selected segments into one: appropriate when segments are too small, adjoining areas will share the same resource and mission type, and the combined workload stays tactically manageable — choose which existing feature's attributes to preserve before confirming.
  • After merging, review: resulting geometry, segment name, preserved attributes, task-force assignment, search metrics, and estimated completion time — a merge can change the workload estimate significantly.
  • Split divides ONE selected segment into two: appropriate when the estimated workload exceeds an operational period or separate squads need separate areas — draw the division line across the polygon and complete the split (this beta build showed Alt+T as a possible Split shortcut — shortcuts may change).
  • Rename BOTH resulting segments immediately with distinct identifiers (e.g., Segment 12104 East / West, or 12104A / 12104B) — edit each polygon individually through Attributes.
  • BETAKnown beta issue: the segment-name field can close before saving after a split — after renaming, reopen Attributes and CONFIRM the new name actually saved; if not, re-enter it and report the bug.
Beta Safeguards — Test Before You Trust It
  • Work in the sandbox unless specifically authorized to use beta tools on live incident data.
  • Confirm the selected incident and the selected-feature count before every edit.
  • Test with a small number of records first; review geometry and attributes after every merge or split.
  • Don't edit another team's data during testing.
  • Independently sanity-check every calculated search metric — don't rely on the beta calculation alone.
  • Report bugs immediately with: application/beta version, date/time, browser, device, incident/environment, feature layer, selected-feature count, exact steps performed, expected vs. actual result, and a screenshot or screen recording.

Troubleshooting SARCOP Maps & Applications

Reporting, diagnosing, and escalating problems across every SARCOP application — Intel Manager, Field Maps, QuickCapture, Dashboards, and Admin Editor.

Source

MA-TF1 TSS SARCOP Field Operations Checklists — Volume 2 (Field Review Draft 0.9). Verify procedures against the SARCOP Training Playlist (v10) before adoption.

📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this page and it will be added here.
  • A vague report costs everyone time: "The map is slow" or "the widget isn't working" gives support nothing to reproduce — state what happened, what should have happened, which application, and which environment.
  • Minimum to report: application (Intel Manager, Field Maps, QuickCapture, Tactical/Strategic Dashboard, Admin Editor); sandbox or deployment; the exact behavior observed; the action that triggered it; a direct link; a screenshot; the approximate date/time.
  • Try to reproduce it first — repeat the action, refresh, reopen the app, and ask another qualified user to try the same workflow before escalating.
  • Unexpected login prompt: capture the prompt, the direct link, which ArcGIS organization it names, which account is currently signed in, and whether more than one ArcGIS session/account is active in the browser — this usually points to a sharing, permission, or authentication conflict, not a broken app.
  • Open browser developer tools (right-click → Inspect): Console tab — screenshot red errors and ignore minor font/resource warnings; Network tab — sort by load time, look for failed or pending requests, and identify the single slowest layer or service.
  • Move from application → web map → layer to isolate it: the visible app can be functioning fine while a supporting layer or map is failing — open the suspected layer's item page directly (Data tab, Visualization tab) to test it on its own.
  • Test the web map by itself: same problem in the plain web map = the map/layer is the issue, not the application; web map fine but the app isn't = suspect app or widget configuration.
  • Lighthouse is a clue, not a verdict: it scores against generic webpage standards and will often rate a complex ArcGIS application poorly even when it's working as intended — use it to spot relative bottlenecks only.
  • Rough load-time reference, not a pass/fail standard: ~3 seconds = normal initial load; ~10 seconds = refresh and retest; ~30 seconds on a familiar component or layer = investigate further.
  • Document it in the issue tracker: observed vs. expected behavior; application/environment/incident; date-time; browser, device, and account context; reproduction steps; troubleshooting already completed; diagnostic findings; attachments — chat messages fix the training-day problem but don't replace this record.
  • During training, don't stall the class: pause, write down the issue, screenshot it, send it to support, switch to a backup workflow, and file the formal report after the session.
Section 11

QuickCapture

One-touch field data collection — setup and sign-in, environment confirmation, user inputs, field collection workflows, and closeout.

Section 11 · QuickCapture

QuickCapture — Setup & Sign-In

ArcGIS QuickCapture provides one-touch data collection for field teams with pre-built buttons for common data entries. This page covers everything before you step off: installing, signing in, device and project prep, user inputs, and the Team Fact Sheet. Field collection workflows continue on QuickCapture — Field Collection & Closeout.

↗
QuickCapture Resources
Configuration files, setup guides, and reference material for the MA-TF1 QuickCapture project.
onedrive.live.com (QuickCapture)
  • Configured buttons: Segment Complete, Victim Located, Hazard Found, Structure Marked
  • Auto-captures GPS location and timestamp on each submission
  • Requires cellular data or Wi-Fi for real-time sync; queues offline
  • Field teams do not need full GIS knowledge — button interface only
  • Submitted records appear on Tactical Dashboard within 2–5 minutes
Source

Checklists below: MA-TF1 TSS SARCOP Field Operations Checklists — Volume 2 (Field Review Draft 0.9). Verify procedures against the SARCOP Training Playlist (v10) before adoption.

Notes Prior to a Deployment

Complete the steps below before deploying — do not wait until you are on scene to install or sign in to the field data collection apps.

  1. Install the apps
    Install ArcGIS QuickCapture and Field Maps on your mobile device (from your app store) in advance of the steps below.

Get the Apps

↗
QuickCapture — Google Play
Android install for ArcGIS QuickCapture.
play.google.com (QuickCapture)
 ↗
ArcGIS QuickCapture — App Store
iPhone install for ArcGIS QuickCapture.
apps.apple.com (QuickCapture)
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Confirm account access. Make sure the SARCOP account is active. No account? Request one through the Program Manager, Task Force Leader, Planning Manager, or TF SARCOP administrator.
  • Sign in to ArcGIS Online. Do NOT select ArcGIS Enterprise unless directed. Failed attempt? Verify the credentials before trying again — repeated attempts lock the account.
  • Configure before issuance. The TIS or Device Manager signs in and configures each cache device before it goes to field personnel. Record which device was issued to each squad.
  • Update the project. Check My Workspace and accept available updates before every operational period.
  • Scan the correct battle-card QR code. RED = training sandbox, BLUE = deployment. Confirm the environment before collecting data — post the current deployment QR at the BoO for rapid onboarding.
  • Confirm permissions and power. Enable location and camera access, turn on Stay Awake when needed, and stage batteries, power banks, cables, and vehicle charging.
  • Configure Auto Send. 15-minute intervals with reliable connectivity, hourly when service is limited. Don't disable it without a strict end-of-shift upload and verification procedure.
  • Check GPS operation. Tap the accuracy indicator to confirm GPS status and current USNG coordinates. Configure and test an external Bluetooth GPS when required.
  • Prepare for offline use. While still connected, open the map and pan/zoom across the response area to cache the basemap, and keep the project open. Request a dedicated offline map for disconnected or multi-period operations.
  • Prove the device. Submit a test record, confirm it reaches the correct SARCOP environment, and remove or identify the test data per procedure.
  • SAFETYDon required PPE before entering the assigned work area.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Select Team Name. Begin typing and select the team from the National US&R catalog. Not listed? Select Other and notify the state SAR/US&R coordinator.
  • Enter the Squad ID. Use the assigned numeric identifier (Blue = 1, Red = 2, Green = 3). Planning maintains the name-to-number crosswalk.
  • Enter the operator Name or identifier required by TF procedure — this identifies the recorder when members don't have individual ArcGIS accounts.
  • Verify inputs at every device handoff. These are saved with the project and attach to every observation.
  • Use Location Sharing only when authorized. It requires an eligible account and may not be available in the standard sandbox. Coordinate with the SARCOP GIS team and test it before deployment.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.

Verify current fields, notification procedures, and update requirements against the deployed Team Fact Sheet.

  • Use the TFS for team-level reporting. The Survey123 form reports team status, location, capability, contacts, and required documents — normally completed by the Plans Team Manager or designated planning representative.
  • Use the correct link. The original public link/QR code creates the initial record. Use the unique link in the confirmation email for every update. Reusing the original link creates a duplicate record.
  • Confirm the environment. Training sandbox for practice, deployment form only for an actual activation.
  • Gather this before opening the form: team name, agency type, personnel count, status, location, ETA, primary contacts, additional POCs, and required forms.
  • Select the correct Team Status.
    Alert — notified and preparing
    Activated — formal activation received
    Demobilized — released or deployment complete
  • Set and verify the map location. Confirm the reverse-geocoded city/ZIP looks reasonable before submitting — this is a status snapshot, not live tracking.
  • Keep Notable Activity concise. Conditions, actions completed, immediate needs, delays, route/location context. No PII, medical details, or law-enforcement-sensitive information.
  • Maintain Transit Status. Select Yes when in transit, and update the ETA when routes, delays, or destinations change.
  • Continue required communications. The TFS does not replace FEMA US&R transit check-ins (including required two-hour check-ins) or agency communications procedures.
  • Prepare attachments before upload. Confirm each document is complete, legible, and named to the incident convention. Example: 1801_20260706_MA-TF1.pdf
  • Review before submitting. Environment, agency type, status, report time, location, transit info, personnel count, contacts, attachments — then confirm the team appears correctly on the TFS Dashboard.

Updates, Reminders & Common Errors

  • Protect the confirmation email. Make sure the primary contact AND an authorized backup can reach the unique update link.
  • Set independent reminders. Don't rely only on automated emails for activation, departure, transit, arrival, or demobilization updates.
  • Update only changed information. Team composition, contacts, personnel count, status, location, ETA, or capability — revise it when it actually changes, not on a schedule.
  • EVERY OP PERIODUpdate the TFS whenever team composition, status, or assignment changes. It feeds the TFS Dashboard, Command's accountability picture.
  • Report duplicate records. Stop using the duplicate, identify the correct record, and notify the IST or SARCOP administrator.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Confirm the correct deployment project is open and the training project is closed.
  • Verify Team Name, Squad ID, and operator Name.
  • Confirm the assigned search type through the chain of command, and read each button's description.
  • Confirm Auto Send, GPS accuracy, location permissions, and camera permissions.
  • Confirm the Team Fact Sheet is current for the operational period.

QuickCapture: Confirm the Correct Environment

QuickCapture uses two separate SARCOP environments:

BLUE = Real World Deployment
RED = Practice Sandbox

Before collecting data, confirm the project title, QR code, and environment color. Your sign-in determines where the data is saved.

Wrong Environment?

If data is entered in the wrong environment, notify the MA-TF1 TIS or PTM immediately.

QuickCapture: First 3 Questions — Answer Once

Select MA-TF1, enter your squad number, provide your full name, and click Done. You can type MA-TF1 in the search bar or scroll to find it.

If prompted to start location sharing, click Start. MA-TF1 phones should already have location sharing enabled.

QuickCapture — Field Collection & Closeout

Field workflows for the operational period: track logs, waypoints, search outcomes, damage observations, photos and PII rules, hazards and support, sync and closeout, troubleshooting, and two-for-one waypoints. Setup and sign-in steps live on QuickCapture — Setup & Sign-In.

Source

MA-TF1 TSS SARCOP Field Operations Checklists — Volume 2 (Field Review Draft 0.9). Verify procedures against the SARCOP Training Playlist (v10) before adoption.

📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Start the assigned track. Select the assigned search-type button and confirm the blinking recording indicator before you move.
  • Use the correct search type.
    Hasty — rapid, life-safety-focused search of the assigned area
    Primary — initial search for occupants or survivors
    Secondary — more detailed, systematic follow-up search
    Specialized — canine, helicopter, boat, or other configured reconnaissance
    Secondary refers to search depth, not a second visit.
  • Start with reliable GPS. Begin outdoors when possible — movement is required to create a meaningful track.
  • Active tracks stay on the device until they're submitted and uploaded.
  • Monitor the map. The green dashed line is your current track — compare it against the assigned segment to spot gaps.
  • End the track when the assignment is complete, you're told to stop, or the mission/search type changes.
  • Complete and submit the record. Stop the track, enter a brief mission comment, and submit.

Doctrine: track logs document WHERE the team searched; waypoints document WHAT the team observed.

📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Use waypoints to record completed searches, survivor status, rescues, evacuations, unmet needs, damage, hazards, support locations, and situation updates.
  • Follow the waypoint workflow: open the category, review the buttons, select the most accurate observation, complete the fields, place the pin on the observed feature, submit.
  • Select by what was observed, not by where the device operator is standing.
  • Use two-for-one waypoint buttons when available to capture the observation and completed search type in one record — full doctrine in the Two-for-One Waypoints group below.
  • Place the pin on the target. The searched structure, hazard, damaged building, or resource location — not the roadway, not the observer.
  • SAFETYOffline: collect the waypoint and place the pin while the map is available. No offline map? Move only as close as safely practical. Never enter an unsafe area solely to improve accuracy.
  • Review waypoint definitions before selecting. The SARCOP Data Dictionary is the authoritative source — use the field quick-reference guide when you need it in the moment.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Searched: use when the assigned search is complete and no victims or unmet needs were identified. Record the search type, structure type, photo/comment if needed, and pin the structure. Example: door knock + window check, nothing found = Searched / Primary Search / Structure.
  • Apply outcome definitions correctly.
    Shelter in Place — person stays at the location, needs no evacuation
    Evacuated — person moved without technical rescue methods
    Rescue — specialized personnel, equipment, or procedures were required
    An evacuation is not a rescue — don't record optimistically.
  • Search Needs Follow-Up: use when additional action is required by the current team, another SAR resource, incident management, or a partner agency.
  • SAFETYLive Victim Confirmed: a live victim is located but the team can't complete the rescue with available resources. Voice-report it immediately — the radio carries the immediate need; the waypoint gives command the shared geographic record.
  • Distinguish victim status.
    Detected — potential indication (canine alert, intelligence report)
    Confirmed — visual, audible, or physical confirmation
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Collect damage observations during wide-area search, reconnaissance, or an assigned damage-observation mission. Read the definitions before selecting a level.
  • Document destroyed or total-loss structures. Select the damage level, add a photo when practical, record whether it was searched, select structure type, add a concise comment, and pin the structure.
  • Don't confuse observation with search. If the team only observed or photographed the structure, select Not Searched so it stays available for a future assignment.
  • Select the closest structure type: single-family, multi-family, commercial, industrial, public/institutional, or other.
  • Mark every structure required by the ROE, including ones observed but not searched — use "Searched per ROE" for cleared sites.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Take operationally useful photos. Show the condition with enough context to interpret it. Use a safe position and avoid unnecessary identifying information — landscape orientation is generally preferred for structure damage.
  • Keep comments brief and objective. Only what's operationally relevant. Review voice-to-text entries before submitting.
  • NEVER enter survivor or victim PII: no names, phone numbers, dates of birth, personal addresses, linked medical details, or identifying photographs, unless specifically authorized through an approved process.
Remember

SARCOP is not a PII system, and it is not a formal Preliminary Damage Assessment tool.

📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Record hazards that may affect responders, survivors, access routes, search operations, or supporting agencies. Select the most accurate type and pin the hazard — not the observer.
  • SAFETYVoice-report life-safety hazards immediately. The app documents the hazard — it is not the alert channel.
  • Pin logistics and support locations accurately, including helispots, command posts, staging areas, drop points, unloading areas, and boat launches.
  • Use Situation Update only when no defined waypoint fits. Add a photo, video, or concise comment as needed — confirm the intended use of incident-specific icons before collecting.
  • Use companion applications when directed. The project may link to Field Maps, Survey123 forms, or incident-specific tools for information standard QuickCapture buttons can't capture.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Send all queued data before closing. Check the upper-right corner for the unsent-data indicator, select Send, and confirm the upload completes.
  • Recover offline records. When connectivity returns, reopen QuickCapture, send queued observations, and verify the unsent-data indicator clears.
  • Confirm receipt before leaving. Verify the data appears on the dashboard or is confirmed by the Documentation Unit or designated SARCOP staff.
  • Complete device closeout. End active tracks, sign out or transfer the device per procedure, and put it on charge.
  • Tag the battery status. Record the device and power-bank charge level, plus date/time, for the next crew.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Cannot sign in: confirm ArcGIS Online is selected, verify credentials, stop before repeated attempts. Refresh sign-in through Profile → My Workspace.
  • Project out of date: open My Workspace, select the project, accept the update.
  • Team missing: select Other and notify the SAR/US&R coordinator.
  • Track contains location spikes: confirm GPS operation and review device location settings — the TIS/GIS specialist can correct invalid segments through the approved workflow.
  • No offline map: open and pan the response area while connected, keep the project open. Request a dedicated offline map for extended disconnected operations.
  • Data not uploading: check connectivity, Auto Send settings, and queued records. Reconnect, send, verify the queue clears.
  • Screen keeps locking: enable Stay Awake, monitor battery, provide charging capability.
  • Reporting a problem: open Profile → About and provide the installed application and project version.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Capture two facts in one record. Supported waypoint types record both what was observed and what search was completed at that location.
  • Use supported waypoint types when configured: Damage Observation, Boat Search, Debris Search, Vehicle Searched, Search per Rules of Engagement.
  • Select the correct Search Type.
    Not Searched — observation collected, search still outstanding
    Reconnaissance — rapid assessment of conditions, access, or hazards
    Hasty — rapid, life-safety-focused search
    Primary — initial systematic search required by the ROE
    Secondary — more thorough, authorized follow-up search
    Other — only when no standard type applies; explain in comments
  • Check for an existing waypoint first. Update the existing feature through the approved workflow instead of creating a duplicate record.
  • Keep Search Type and Follow-Up Status separate: Search Type records the work completed; Follow-Up Status identifies whether more action is needed.
  • Turn on both map layers.
    Waypoints — observation symbol
    Waypoint Search Type — search-status halo
  • Use the legend. Don't rely on color memory alone — especially with black-and-white printing, poor screens, low light, or color-vision limitations.
  • Complete the full record: select the waypoint type, confirm location, complete observation fields, select search type, add photos/comments, set follow-up status, submit.
  • Verify after submission. Confirm the waypoint, search-status halo, pop-up information, and follow-up status are correct — use the approved editing workflow for corrections.
  • Search Type field missing? Confirm the waypoint supports the field, the project is current, and the correct incident is open. Refresh or reopen before contacting the TIS.
Section 12

Field Maps

ArcGIS Field Maps — map review, navigation, offline areas, editing, RST/HAS structure triage, and sync workflows.

Section 12 · Field Maps

Field Maps

ArcGIS Field Maps provides full GIS editing capabilities for advanced users. Used by Team Leaders and GIS-trained personnel.

↗
Field Maps Resources
Configuration files, offline map packages, and setup guides for the MA-TF1 Field Maps project.
1drv.ms (Field Maps)
  • Download offline map area before deployment in areas with limited connectivity
  • Edit feature geometries and attributes directly on map
  • Attach photos to any feature record
  • Sync changes manually when connectivity is restored
  • Inspect layer sync status before departing base
Source

Checklists below: MA-TF1 TSS SARCOP Field Operations Checklists — Volume 2 (Field Review Draft 0.9). Verify procedures against the SARCOP Training Playlist (v10) before adoption.

📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Install ArcGIS Field Maps from the device app store. Allow location access WHILE USING the app — deny it and navigation/current-location tools won't work.
  • Confirm account access. Public training maps may open without sign-in; secure deployment maps require ArcGIS Online (NOT Enterprise unless directed). Verify the user can open the map before issuing the device.
  • Open the correct SARCOP map through QuickCapture → Additional Apps → Field Maps Online, the approved QR code, or the current map name/short code — get codes from the battle card or map author, not from memory.
  • Add the required maps to Favorites, including the online and offline-capable versions — faster to reopen and prevents opening the wrong one.
  • Confirm the environment. Verify training versus deployment before continuing — same discipline as the RED/BLUE battle-card QR codes.
  • Use each application for its purpose: QuickCapture collects tracks and waypoints fast; Field Maps reviews, navigates, filters, and interacts with the common operating picture — including work by your team, other teams, and supporting agencies.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Open the dedicated offline-capable map. Not every SARCOP web map supports offline use — reach it through Additional Apps, its QR code, or its short code.
  • Download preplanned areas over reliable Wi-Fi. Stagger downloads when prepping multiple devices — a room of devices pulling at once saturates the network.
  • Create an on-demand offline area through ⋯ → Add Offline Area when you need one. Keep the extent as small as practical and give it an operational name (e.g., Division A) — it exists on your device only.
  • Test the area in airplane mode. Confirm the map, required layers, and the full anticipated operational area display with no signal.
  • Don't rely on offline areas for photographs. Image attachments may not be included in the offline package — never assume the offline map holds the pictures behind an observation.
  • Sync at the start and end of every operational period when connectivity is available — check for pending edits or sync errors afterward.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Select a search segment to review status, assigned team, responsible squad, and search priority.
  • Use the legend before interpreting symbols, colors, or line styles. Still unsure? Select the feature and read its description.
  • Use Bookmarks to jump to predefined incident areas, divisions, municipalities, islands, or priority search areas.
  • Verify imagery date and source. Imagery Hybrid normally shows pre-disaster conditions — post-event imagery may be a separate operational layer.
  • Select features to read their records. Tracks show team, squad, search type, and time; waypoints show observations, outcomes, damage, comments, and available photos.
  • Review stacked features carefully. Select the correct item from the list before acting or editing.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Search for an address or place to locate command posts, staging areas, structures, or access points. Recenter with the GPS icon when done.
  • Use the built-in compass for short-distance or foot navigation. Directions opens an external navigation app.
  • Keep Field Maps available. When switching applications, don't force-close it.
  • SAFETYVerify navigation routes against current conditions. External applications may route personnel onto blocked, unsafe, or impassable roads.
  • Use Measure for planning estimates. Distance and area results don't account for terrain, debris, water, damaged roads, or actual travel routes.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Use Markup for temporary routes, meeting points, areas of concern, and field notes.
  • Treat markup as device-local information. It is not official operational data and does not replace waypoints, tracks, segments, or hazard reports.
  • Share only the approved map link. Confirm training versus deployment and that the recipient has the required access and credentials.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Use the Layers panel to display only what the assignment requires — segments, tracks, outcomes, hazards, facilities. Turning a layer off changes only your display; nothing is deleted.
  • Filter layers by team, squad, search type, status, operational period, or damage level.
  • Confirm filtered results are reasonable before making an operational decision.
  • Clear filters when finished. Before concluding data is missing, confirm: correct map, correct area, layer visible, filter cleared, recent sync completed.
  • Never conclude a feature is absent while its layer is turned off.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Edit only when authorized. Permissions vary by map, layer, account, and assigned role.
  • Correct misplaced waypoints carefully. Confirm the record, select Edit, move the point to the actual structure or observed feature, save, and verify the change.
  • Correct attributes only after confirming the original observation. Stay within your team's recent records unless cross-team coordination has occurred.
  • Do not delete suspected duplicates or unusual records. Refer them to the TIS, Plans Team Manager, or GIS specialist.
  • Do not reshape or delete track-log spikes. Track corrections belong to TIS or GIS personnel.
  • Continue routine data collection through QuickCapture. Add features directly in Field Maps only when the workflow requires it and you're trained and authorized.
  • Coordinate edits. The last synchronized edit may overwrite earlier changes to the same feature.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Copy an existing boundary into the search-segment layer. Turn on the reference layer, select the correct polygon, choose Copy → Copy All, and confirm the destination layer.
  • Inspect the copied geometry. Correct stray vertices, gaps, and unintended overlaps before submitting.
  • Complete the segment attributes: status, segment name, mission type, assigned team, operational period, and priority when known.
  • Review all copied values. A matched field may not contain the correct operational value.
  • Confirm the segment is tactically manageable. Consider size, structure count, terrain, access, and available resources.
  • Follow the incident approval process for field-created segments and avoid duplicate or overlapping boundaries.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Keep default settings unless the TIS or GIS specialist directs a change to GPS averaging, location providers, cellular sync, or accuracy controls.
  • Set coordinate display to USNG. Include grid zone and required precision, and use read-back confirmation when transmitting coordinates.
  • Use the approved photo size. Smaller images reduce upload time, cellular use, and storage demands.
  • Test external Bluetooth GNSS equipment outdoors before deployment. Pair the receiver (e.g., Bad Elf, Arrow, Trimble), select it as the provider, and verify position and accuracy — recreational units like a Garmin 60-series can't serve as a provider and may not beat a modern phone.
  • Prevent battery optimization from suspending Field Maps. Allow required background GPS, tracking, and synchronization.
  • Use Location Sharing only when licensed and authorized. Confirm the account, layer, and incident workflow, and avoid duplicate sharing processes.
  • Manage screen use. Reduce brightness, turn the screen off between checks, and avoid keeping navigation continuously displayed.
  • Use airplane mode only after the offline map is loaded and when the communications plan permits — GPS should keep functioning.
  • Carry portable power and use vehicle charging. Charge and sync devices at the end of the shift.
  • Test application updates on one cache device before updating the full device inventory.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Use RST to prioritize structures, not to complete a structural assessment. Consider survivor probability, operational risk, and estimated victim-access effort.
  • Open the dedicated RST QuickCapture project. Use the approved QR code from the Worksite Triage Dashboard or current job aid.
  • Confirm training versus deployment by reviewing the project title before collecting data.
  • Complete Project User Inputs: Team Name, Squad number, and collector name or approved identifier.
  • Review the linked RST resources before operational use — current occupancy definitions, guidance, and training materials.
  • Select the probability and risk combination that best reflects the observed conditions — nine buttons, Probability of Survivors × Operational Risk.
    Probability may consider: occupancy, time of day, witnesses, signs of presence, survivable voids, damage, prior search information
    Risk may consider: instability, collapse potential, falling debris, fire, hazardous materials, utilities, flooding, access limitations
  • Follow the capture workflow: select the probability/risk button, add a photo, select suspected occupancy, estimate victim-access effort, add notes, place the pin, submit.
  • SAFETYTake a wide landscape photo when practical. Show overall damage, collapse configuration, access, hazards, and possible survivable spaces without entering an unsafe position.
  • Select occupancy type based on evidence, including signage, building characteristics, local knowledge, and witness information.
  • Estimate victim-access effort. Consider entrapment depth, collapse pattern, access, breaching, shoring, heavy equipment, personnel, and time.
  • Explain the rating in the notes. Survivor indicators, estimated number or location, witnesses, voids, access limitations, hazards, resource needs — objective and PII-free.
  • Place the pin on the structure footprint. Not the roadway, not the observer's position.
  • Keep the process rapid. Collect enough to prioritize the structure, then continue the assignment.

RST does not replace detailed structural evaluation, hazard assessment, technical search, worksite assessment, or engineer review.

📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Use Field Maps to review and expand RST records and complete the linked Hazard Assessment and Structural evaluation record.
  • Use the correct personnel: trained responders may collect RST; the detailed hazard evaluation must be completed by qualified and assigned personnel.
  • Confirm the correct record before editing. Select the structure point and verify whether the pop-up is the RST or HAS record.
  • Recognize pending HAS records. A gray HAS point means the hazard evaluation hasn't been completed.
  • Complete only known and operationally useful RST details. Don't guess to populate optional fields.
  • Answer the qualified Structure Specialist field accurately. Select Yes only when the evaluator meets applicable qualification requirements.
  • Enter the HAS evaluator's Team, Squad, and Name, even when different from the original RST collector.
  • Assign the structure risk rating based on observed structural conditions, collapse potential, access and egress, falling objects, utilities, fire, hazardous materials, and secondary hazards.
  • Complete applicable structural fields, including structural system, lateral system, basement, interior and exterior hazards, and stabilization needs.
  • Document access and strategy information for the next team — recommended entry points, areas to avoid, shoring or monitoring needs, safe zones, collapse zones, and specialized resources.
  • Apply the full HAS 3 checklist before acknowledging completion. Record exceptions and unresolved concerns.
  • Confirm the address and USNG when available. Don't delay the assessment solely because an address is missing.
  • Verify the mapped structure. The USNG or mapped point may serve as the structure identifier.
  • Interpret linked symbols correctly. The RST symbol shows triage classification; the HAS symbol shows the completed hazard or structural risk category.
  • Prioritize essential fields and submit. Add secondary detail later when operational tempo permits.
  • Use the operational sequence: RST → Prioritize → Allocate Resources → HAS for Entry Structures → Reassess
  • SAFETYVoice-report immediate life-safety hazards first, including structural movement, energized utilities, hazardous materials, fire, or rising water — document them afterward.
📷 Image placeholder — send a screenshot or visual for this group and it will be added here.
  • Sync Field Maps when connectivity returns. Confirm pending edits are zero and updated RST/HAS symbols appear correctly.
  • Report unresolved segments, blocked routes, pending evaluations, and open hazards during the operational handoff.
  • Refresh or remove offline areas per procedure so the next operational period begins with current data.
  • Confirm all edits are synchronized before closing the application or transferring the device.
Section 13

Mapping Tools

Reconnaissance documentation and mapping tools — sketch maps, field notes, georeferenced reporting, handheld GPS, mobile field apps, and UAV recon, plus land navigation fundamentals and coordinate systems.

Section 13 · Mapping Tools
Section 13 — Mapping Tools

Sketch Maps: Simple Lines, Critical Clarity

Sketch maps remain one of the fastest and most universal reconnaissance & search tools. They require no technology and can be created by any responder in the field, yet their value depends entirely on accuracy, clarity, and standards that prevent misinterpretation.

Guiding Principle
"Any responder who picks this up must be able to read it the same way."
All 8 Components Required

A sketch map missing any of the components below is not operationally valid — treat every one as mandatory, not optional.

To be operationally valid, every sketch map must include these key components:

  • Title: Identify the area, purpose, team, and operational period.
  • North Arrow: Show map orientation.
  • Scale: Include a scale or the notation "Not to Scale."
  • Legend: Define all symbols, colors, and abbreviations.
  • Map Author: Include the author's name, agency, and contact information.
  • Date and Time: Record the date and time of creation or most recent update.
  • Coordinates/Address: Include coordinates and a street address, when available, to improve spatial accuracy.
  • Building Sides: Label Side A as the front, followed clockwise by Sides B, C, and D.
↗
Download Blank Sketch Map Template
Printable field template with fields for the required components above — title, north arrow, scale, legend, author, date/time, coordinates, and building sides.
1drv.ms (Sketch Map Template)
Color Coding Standard

Use simple, consistent symbols and apply uniform color coding so any reader can interpret the map at a glance: blue for water, green for vegetation, brown for structures/buildings, orange for hazards, and red for victim or clue locations.

Sketch Maps Are Living Documents

As operations evolve, update sketch maps regularly. To remove information, draw a single line through it and record the date and time of deletion. When edits begin to affect readability, redraw or digitize a clean version. Photograph or scan each sketch and upload it to the COP, transforming a field drawing into a shared, durable source of mission intelligence.

MA-TF1 Sketch Map Tool

Victim Markings

Click a marking below, then click on the map to place it. Confirmed Alive and Confirmed Deceased will prompt for a victim count. These follow the standard FEMA field marking convention (red, per the "V" system) — team insignia and the directional-distance arrow variant are left for hand-drawing, since those vary per operational period.

Multi-Point Line & Freehand Shape

Circle, Triangle, Diamond, and Arrow work like Rectangle — drag from one corner/end to the other. Multi-Point Line is different: click once per point along the path, then tap Finish Path (or press Esc) to end it. Freehand Shape works the same way but closes into a filled shape — click for straight edges, click-and-drag for curves, then either click back near your starting point or tap Finish Path to close it. The Fill control (None / Semi / Solid) sets how Rectangle, Circle, Triangle, Diamond, and Freehand Shape render — Void Space always stays its own fixed translucent pink regardless of this setting. House and Tree drop at a fixed size wherever you click, in whatever color is currently selected (or your own uploaded image — see Manage Icons below).

Tool
Color
Width
Fill
Victim Marks
Map Symbols
Search Tools
Symbol Size
🖼️ Manage Map Symbol Icons — replace House/Tree with your own image, or add new symbol types
House Using default line art
Tree Using default line art
Color Coding Standard

Blue for water, green for vegetation, brown for structures/buildings, orange for hazards, and red for victim or clue locations.

Section 13 — Mapping Tools

Field Notes

Field notes form the audit trail of reconnaissance/search. They transform observations into verifiable records that support accuracy, accountability, and continuity between operational periods. Unlike informal jotting, these notes create the quality check in every operation, giving commanders and GIS staff confidence that the geospatial field data can be trusted, verified, and acted upon.

Guiding Question
"Could the next team pick this up and trust it completely?"
Not Informal Jotting

Field notes are a quality check on every operation — legible, structured, and photographed for redundancy.

To be operationally valid, every field notebook entry must include these key components:

  • An incident header (incident name, date, squad ID, operational period)
  • Sequential entries that capture observations in chronological order
  • GPS coordinates where available to anchor notes geospatially
  • A clear description of hazards, access issues, or survivability clues
  • Witness or contact references if applicable
  • The author's name and agency
  • A date and time stamp for each entry

At handover, critical observations are converted into COP features such as hazard waypoints, or tracks.

Important

The ICS 214 Activity Log's role varies by organization. Some operations require a formal 214 for every unit, while others use it in place of field notes, or combine field notes later. In some cases, only field notes are kept. The Authority Having Jurisdiction (AHJ) determines the documentation standard.

↗
ICS 214 Activity Log
Blank ICS 214 Activity Log form for units that require or supplement formal documentation alongside field notes.
1drv.ms (ICS 214 Activity Log)
Image placeholder — reference image to be added from GitHub
Discipline In, Accountability Out

By enforcing discipline in field documentation — whether through field notes, ICS 214s, or both — SAR operations maintain an audit trail that supports decision-making and ensures accountability.

Section 13 — Mapping Tools

Georeferenced Reporting

Georeferenced reporting transforms words into locations, bridging the gap between spoken or written observations and actionable geospatial intelligence. Reconnaissance often begins with radio calls, text messages, or written notes such as "200 yards south of the bridge" or "near the collapsed barn." On their own, these descriptions can be vague, inconsistent, or subject to misinterpretation. To be operationally useful, they must be anchored to physical landmarks and, whenever possible, precise coordinates.

Guiding Principle
From subjective to objective — every observation must be traceable.
Not Assumption

"I saw something there" becomes "Verified point at X,Y, medium confidence" — with a name, a time, and a confidence rating behind it.

Once received, GIS staff convert these descriptions into provisional waypoints in the COP. Each entry must include:

  • Reporter's name and affiliation
  • Time and date of report
  • Location reference (landmark or coordinate)
  • Brief description of observation
  • Confidence rating (high, medium, or low)
Low-Confidence Points

If precision is low or the description ambiguous, the point is flagged for verification and follow-up reconnaissance. This prevents false assumptions while preserving potentially valuable data.

Image placeholder — reference image to be added from GitHub
Intelligence From Any Source

Georeferenced reporting captures intelligence quickly from any source — first responders, local witnesses, or specialized teams — and converts it into structured, geospatially anchored data, ensuring the Common Operating Picture reflects reality with traceable accuracy.

Section 13 — Mapping Tools

Mobile Field Apps: Real-Time Intel at Your Fingertips

Mobile applications such as QuickCapture and Field Maps, used on phones and tablets, represent the highest-fidelity method for reconnaissance data collection. Their strength lies in reducing the gap between what is seen in the field and what becomes visible in the Common Operating Picture (COP).

Guiding Principle
Capture fast, analyze later.
Division of Labor

Field teams capture observations. GIS, Tech Info, and Intel staff apply confidence ratings and analysis afterward.

A single entry in a mobile app can generate a waypoint or track line, allowing responders to mark hazards, access routes, or observed clues with precision. In many cases, the waypoint or track speaks for itself — but when an observation is ambiguous, a brief description should be added, and photos add visual context that words alone cannot capture.

🕒 Automatic Metadata
User, date, time, and device information are captured automatically, reducing responder burden and ensuring consistency.
📶 Offline Mode
Teams must be trained to operate with preloaded maps and cached forms whenever network coverage isn't guaranteed.
Sync Verification Required

During or after periods of limited or no connection, a sync verification must confirm that all records have uploaded correctly once connectivity is restored. This prevents data gaps and ensures the COP reflects the most complete picture possible.

Image placeholder — reference image to be added from GitHub
The Fastest Path to Actionable Intelligence

When properly implemented, mobile apps provide the fastest and most connected pathway for turning reconnaissance into actionable intelligence. Hazards, access constraints, and clues appear in near real time within the COP, allowing commanders to adapt plans quickly and align resources with verified ground truth.

Section 13 — Mapping Tools

UAV Recon: Expanding the View from Above

UAVs/drones extend reconnaissance by providing a rapid aerial perspective that ground teams cannot achieve. They can quickly reveal terrain, hazard zones, blocked routes, and possible movement corridors, helping responders and command staff visualize the operating environment before committing resources.

Guiding Principle
UAVs extend the view — they do not replace the ground team.
Complement, Not Substitute

Aerial imagery provides broad context; confirmation and detailed observations still come from field teams.

For UAV reconnaissance to be operationally valid, imagery must be time-stamped, georeferenced, and tied to a specific tasking. Captured photos, video, or live feeds can be imported into the Common Operating Picture (COP) as geotagged image layers, or streamed directly to the command post for real-time decision-making.

🗺️ Orthomosaic Product
A stitched, geometrically corrected composite of many overlapping UAV images, aligned to the earth's surface so it can be used like a map. Orthorectification removes tilt and terrain distortions, enabling distance and area measurements not possible from raw aerial photos — produced rapidly in post-flight processing and overlaid in GIS as a high-resolution basemap.
Operational Limitations

UAV operations require approval from the Authority Having Jurisdiction (AHJ) and must comply with Rules of Engagement (ROE) and FAA/airspace regulations. They are also constrained by line-of-sight requirements and weather — strong winds, rain, fog, or low visibility can severely limit flight effectiveness.

Image placeholder — reference image to be added from GitHub
A Force Multiplier

When integrated correctly, UAVs act as a force multiplier, extending the reach of reconnaissance, reducing risk to responders, and providing geospatially anchored imagery products that strengthen the Common Operating Picture.

How to Use a Compass

Section 13 — Mapping Tools

How to Use a GPS — Garmin GPSMAP 62 Series

Field Note
MA-TF1 uses QuickCapture to collect waypoints.
GPS as Backup / Redundancy

GPS units are often used in the field as a backup or redundancy to track movement. Ensure they are turned on at the start of a search or recon, and turned off and given to TIS upon return.

💡 Click any task below to expand it and see the steps.
Getting Started
Install / Replace the Batteries
  1. Remove the battery cover: turn the D-ring 1/4 turn counterclockwise and pull up.
  2. Insert two AA batteries (alkaline, NiMH, or lithium), observing polarity.
  3. Replace the battery cover.
After installing new batteries, update the battery type in Setup > System > Battery Type for an accurate battery-life reading.
Turn On, Set Language, Acquire Satellites
  1. Press and hold the power button to turn the device on or off.
  2. On first use, use the Rocker to select a language.
  3. Go outdoors to an open area — the device searches for satellites automatically (typically 30–60 seconds).
  4. A question mark flashes while the device determines your location; GPS bars turn green once signal is acquired.
Know the Buttons
  1. FIND — open the search menu (waypoints, tracks, routes, addresses).
  2. MARK — save your current location as a waypoint.
  3. QUIT — cancel or return to the previous menu/page.
  4. ENTER — select an option / acknowledge a message.
  5. MENU — open the options menu for the current page; press twice to open the main menu from any page.
  6. PAGE — cycle through the main pages (Map, Compass, Main Menu, Trip Computer).
  7. Rocker — navigate menus and move the map cursor.
  8. IN / OUT — zoom in / out on the map.
Waypoints
Create a Waypoint
  1. From any page, press MARK.
  2. Select Done to save it at your current location.
Navigate to a Waypoint
  1. Press FIND > Waypoints.
  2. Select the waypoint, then select Go.
Edit a Waypoint
  1. From the main menu, select Waypoint Manager.
  2. Select the waypoint, then the attribute to change (name, location, symbol).
  3. Enter the new information and select Done.
Delete a Waypoint
  1. From the main menu, select Waypoint Manager.
  2. Select the waypoint, press MENU, then select Delete.
Routes
Create a Route
  1. From the main menu, select Route Planner > Create Route > Select First Point.
  2. Select a category, then the first point; select Use.
  3. Select Select Next Point and repeat until the route is complete (minimum 2 points).
  4. Press QUIT to save the route.
Edit or Rename a Route
  1. From the main menu, select Route Planner, then select the route.
  2. Select Edit Route to reorder (Move Up/Down), Insert, or Remove points.
  3. Select Change Name to rename; enter the new name and select Done.
Navigate, Reverse, or Stop a Route
  1. To navigate: press FIND > Routes, select the route, then Go.
  2. To reverse direction: select the route > Reverse Route > select it again > View Map > Go.
  3. To stop navigating: press FIND > Stop Navigation.
View or Delete a Route
  1. From the main menu, select Route Planner, then the route.
  2. Select View Map to see it on the map, or Delete Route to remove it.
  3. Select Active Route from the main menu to review points on the route you're currently navigating.
Tracks
Set Up Track Log Recording
  1. From the main menu, select Setup > Tracks > Track Log.
  2. Select Record, Show On Map (draws a line on the map as you move) or Record, Do Not Show.
  3. Select Record Method: Distance, Time, or Auto (variable rate for optimal detail).
  4. Select Recording Interval — Most Often gives the most detail but fills memory fastest.
View, Save, or Clear the Current Track
  1. From the main menu, select Track Manager > Current Track.
  2. Select View Map or Elevation Plot to review it.
  3. Select Save Track (entire track) or Save Portion (a section) to keep it.
  4. To clear: Setup > Reset > Clear Current Track > Yes.
Navigate or Delete a Saved Track
  1. To navigate: press FIND > Tracks, select a saved track, then Go.
  2. To delete: Track Manager, select the track, then Delete > Yes.
Compass & Navigation
Calibrate the Compass
  1. Calibrate outdoors, away from vehicles, buildings, or power lines that affect magnetic fields.
  2. From the compass page, press MENU > Calibrate Compass > Start, then follow the on-screen instructions.
  3. Recalibrate after long moves, temperature changes, or a battery swap.
Navigate with the Compass
  1. Press PAGE to reach the compass, hold the device level.
  2. Follow the bearing pointer toward your destination — when it points straight up, you're on course.
Use Sight 'N Go
  1. From the main menu, select Sight 'N Go.
  2. Point the device at a distant object (e.g. a landmark), then select Lock Direction > Set Course.
  3. Follow the compass to navigate toward it — useful for holding a bearing on a visible feature without a saved waypoint.
Measure Distance / Change Map Orientation
  1. On the map, press MENU > Measure Distance; move the cursor to the target point to read the distance at the bottom of the screen.
  2. To change orientation: MENU > Setup Map > Orientation — North Up, Track Up, or Automotive Mode.
Field-Relevant Settings
Set Position Format to USNG/MGRS
  1. From the main menu, select Setup > Position Format.
  2. Set Position Format to the grid your team is using.
  3. Leave Map Datum and Map Spheroid at their defaults (WGS 84) unless a specific chart calls for something else.
MA-TF1 operations run on USNG/MGRS, not lat/long — confirm this setting before deployment.
Select Battery Type
  1. Setup > System > Battery Type > select Alkaline, Lithium, or Rechargeable NiMH.
Use lithium batteries in below-freezing conditions — alkaline capacity drops sharply in cold and can rupture at high heat.
Extend Battery Life
  1. Setup > Display > Battery Save > On.
  2. Lower the backlight brightness and shorten Backlight Timeout (Setup > Display > Backlight Timeout) — screen backlighting is the biggest battery drain.
Calculate an Area
  1. From the main menu, select Area Calculation > Start.
  2. Walk the perimeter of the area, then select Calculate.
  3. You can save the resulting track and change the unit of measure afterward.
Set a Proximity Alarm
  1. From the main menu, select Proximity Alarms > Create New.
  2. Select a category and point, select Use, enter a radius, then Done.
Reset Between Operational Periods
  1. Setup > Reset lets you clear the current track, reset trip data, delete all waypoints, or restore all defaults — individually.
There's no single "end of op period" shortcut — reset only what you intend to.
Camera (GPSMAP 62sc / 62stc only)
Take a Photo
  1. From the main menu, select Camera.
  2. Turn the device horizontally or vertically to set orientation; press IN/OUT to zoom.
  3. Hold ENTER to focus, release ENTER to capture.
