The photo's GPS location is saved automatically and can be navigated to like a waypoint.
Use Low Light Mode
  1. From the Camera page, press MENU > Low Light Mode.
View Photos
  1. From the main menu, select Photo Viewer; use the Rocker to scroll, select a photo for a larger view.
  2. Press MENU > Sort Photos to sort by Most Recently, Near a Location, or On a Specific Date.
Navigate to a Photo's Location
  1. Photo Viewer > select the photo > MENU > View Map > Go.
  2. Navigate using the map or compass as usual.
Delete a Photo
  1. Photo Viewer > select the photo > MENU > Delete > Yes.
↗
GPS Garmin 62 Series Operating Manual
Full owner's manual (PDF) — specs, complete data field reference, all settings, and troubleshooting not covered above.
1drv.ms (GPS Garmin 62 Series Manual)

Reading a Topographic Map

USNG Reader

No USNG reader content added yet. Send the reference material or instructions and this page will be built out.

Coordinate Systems

No coordinate systems content added yet. Send the reference material (MGRS, UTM, Lat/Long, etc.) and this page will be built out.

Section 16

Reference & Forms

Quick-reference acronyms, standard forms, technical manuals, deployment references, and packing lists.

Section 16 · Reference & Forms

Deployment Inprocessing

Reference maps for team inprocessing and cache setup upon arrival at a mobilization or staging location.

Point of Assembly Check-In Process

Upon arriving at the MA-TF1 site located at 43 Airport in Beverly, Massachusetts, members are required to drop off their bags at Building 9, park their vehicles in the designated area, sign in, drop off their vehicle keys, and verify their contact information and emergency contact information at Building 14. Once these steps are completed, they can move on to process all the remaining areas and conclude by checking in with their discipline manager. In the event of an incident that requires extra security, ID badges will be checked at the gatehouse.

Inprocessing Map

Cache Map

Inprocessing Checklist

Checklist reference for personnel inprocessing during deployment activation.

Check-in Areas

The first Tech Info Specialist, Planning Team Manager, or designee will provide each station with an iPhone to scan IDs or leave T-Cards in Building 14 in the event of no connectivity. Additionally, laptop computers will be provided to HazMat and Medical in Building 14 and Comms in Building 7A.

The Office Manager or their designee will leave an Emergency Contact List, Member Contact List and Sign-in sheet in Building 14.

If a deployed member is required to staff a station, they will be given priority in processing all other stations.

In rare cases, Logistics may add a station based on an incident-specific need. If an additional station is added to the processing line, people will be informed during their sign-in at Building 14.

If members have not already done so, they must scan the Telegram QR Code located in Building 14 upon arrival at the Cache to receive critical information through the Deployment Telegram Account. DuoDote will be issued in Building 6 if needed.

Acronyms & Terms

AcronymDefinition
COPCommon Operating Picture
GISGeographic Information System
HDOPHorizontal Dilution of Precision (GPS accuracy metric)
ICIncident Commander
IDLHImmediately Dangerous to Life or Health
LASTLocate, Access, Stabilize, Transport
LCESLookouts, Communications, Escape Routes, Safety Zones
LELLower Explosive Limit
LUNARLocation, Unit, Nature, Air/Resources, Resources Needed (MAYDAY format)
MAYDAYDistress signal — firefighter / rescuer in emergency
PARPersonnel Accountability Report
PPEPersonal Protective Equipment
PTTPush to Talk
RIGRapid Intervention Group
RITRapid Intervention Team
SARCOPSearch and Rescue Common Operating Picture
SCBASelf-Contained Breathing Apparatus
US&RUrban Search and Rescue
USARUrban Search and Rescue (alternate)
WGS84World Geodetic System 1984 (GPS coordinate standard)

Forms & Reports

Two ways to use this page: pull a blank form from one of the libraries below, or check the quick reference to see which form applies to a given situation.

Form Libraries

↗
ICS Fillable Forms
FEMA's full library of standard Incident Command System forms (ICS-201 through ICS-225+), fillable PDF format.
training.fema.gov/icsresource/icsforms.aspx
 ↗
US&R System Forms
National US&R System-level forms folder (OneDrive). Task Force administrative, logistics, and operational forms.
onedrive.live.com (US&R System)
 ↗
MA-TF1 & Tech Search Forms
Task Force-specific and Technical Search forms folder (OneDrive). Victim reports, structure surveys, equipment logs.
1drv.ms (MA-TF1 / Tech Search)

Common Forms Quick Reference

ICS-204 — Assignment List
Team assignments for each operational period. Completed by Branch Director. Issued to all Team Leaders before deployment.
ICS-214 — Unit Log
Chronological activity log for each operational unit. Maintained throughout operational period. Archived at incident close.
Victim Report
MA-TF1 victim documentation form. Records victim location, condition, interventions, transport destination, and receiving provider.
Structure Survey Form
Rapid structural assessment record. Collapse type, hazards, access/egress routes, search findings. Filed per structure per operational period.
Equipment Accountability Log
All technical search tools tracked by serial number. In/out times, team responsible, and condition on return.
Operational Period Briefing
Standard briefing format: situation update, objectives, assignments, safety message, communications plan, logistics notes.

Team Roles & Responsibilities

Search Team Leader
Responsible for team safety, assignment execution, PAR, communications, and SARCOP updates. Two-person minimum under command at all times.
Technical Search Specialist
Deploys and interprets acoustic, seismic, and camera tools. Trained to FEMA Tech Search Specialist level. Reports findings to Team Leader.
Search Branch Director
Manages all search operations across sectors. Assigns segments. Reviews coverage. Reports to Operations Section Chief.
Safety Officer
Independent authority to halt any unsafe operation. Conducts continuous hazard monitoring. Reports directly to IC.
GIS / Documentation Specialist
Maintains SARCOP, Field Maps, and COP. Coordinates data collection from field teams. Produces Tactical Dashboard.
Medical Specialist
EMT or Paramedic assigned to search team. Provides victim stabilization, rehab monitoring, and personnel medical surveillance.

Cache Phone List

Contact reference for cache personnel and points of contact.

Technical Manuals

Equipment and system manuals used by MA-TF1 Technical Search.

↗
GPS Garmin 62 Series Operating Manual
Full operating manual (PDF) for the Garmin GPSMAP 62 series handheld GPS units.
1drv.ms (GPS Garmin 62 Series Manual)
More Coming

Send additional titles/links (listening device manuals, camera systems, radios, etc.) and they'll be added here as link cards.

Deployment Types / Org Charts

Reference for MA-TF1 deployment configurations and the associated organizational structure for each. Click a type below to expand its details and chart.

Type-1 US&R Task Force — A Type-1 Urban Search and Rescue Task Force is a 70-person multi-disciplined resource which conducts search and rescue response efforts for all hazards, including locating, accessing, medically stabilizing, and extricating survivors from light through heavy frame construction damaged structures. Specialized operational personnel include rescue, canine search, medical, hazardous materials (hazmat), technical search, and communications. A Type-1 task force is capable of continuous 24-hour operations, which can split into two 12-hour operational periods.

Type-3 US&R Task Force — Consists of a 35-person multi-disciplined team with the same core capabilities as a Type-2 US&R task force, capable of 12-hour operational periods for up to 14 days.

Type-4 US&R Task Force — Consists of a 22-person team with similar core capabilities as a Type-3 US&R task force, with the focus of operations on light to moderate frame construction damaged structures, capable of 12-hour operational periods for up to 14 days.

Mission Ready Packages (MRP)

Smaller, specialized packages capable of independent deployment without a full task force activation.

Packing List

Personal and team gear checklist for deployment.

More Coming

Send additional items or categories (PPE, tools, personal gear, medical, etc.) if you'd like this expanded into an interactive checklist matching the style used in Section 3.

Photos & Media

MA-TF1 Media & Photo Policy — governs all photos/video taken relative to an activation or official FEMA event, plus guidance for members interviewed by media during or after a disaster.

⚠ Ownership

All media (photos/videos) taken relative to an activation or official FEMA event are the property of FEMA. Failure to adhere to this policy will result in disciplinary action.

Order of Precedence

This policy applies unless superseded by, in order:

  1. Rules of Engagement (ROE)
    Set forth specific to each deployment. Provided by the Incident Support Team (IST) in the Operational or Incident Action Plan (OAP/IAP), or through other means when applicable or time-sensitive.
  2. Authority Having Jurisdiction (AHJ)
    Policies or guidance issued by the local jurisdiction.
  3. FEMA Directives, Policies, or Guidance
  4. HIPAA
    Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act requirements.

Standing Order

  • Unless otherwise instructed by the IST Public Information Officer (PIO) or their designee, MA-TF1 may distribute general media without prior IST PIO approval — limited to the cache, past deployments, pre-deployment activities, deploying the cache, etc.
  • No specific media about the mission, deployment, geographical details (e.g. convoy or destination locations), or other task forces/partners shall be provided.

Media Categories

Sensitive Nature
  • Includes human remains, victim faces, or other items deemed sensitive or not for public distribution.
  • Captured only by designated personnel for official documentation, adhering to ROE and AHJ guidance.
  • Transferred to authorized personnel and immediately deleted from phones/cameras as soon as possible.
  • Not for public distribution — saved only in password-protected folders.
Operational
  • All media taken by any team member during an operational period that doesn't fall under Sensitive Nature.
  • Primary objective: document critical activities, task force capabilities, operations, environmental conditions, damage, etc.
  • All members give due regard to those impacted by the disaster — avoid posing, loitering, or other actions perceived as disrespectful.
  • Before submitting to the IST PIO or designee: the Technical Information Specialist (TIS)/Plans personnel (or designee) conduct a cursory check using the MA-TF1 Photo/Video Review Checklist, confirming ROE, HIPAA, and AHJ/FEMA compliance.
  • As necessary, Safety personnel or their designee screen media for actual or perceived safety violations.
  • Media that doesn't meet the criteria above may be used for internal training and education.
Off-Duty / Downtime
  • All other media that doesn't fall under Sensitive Nature or Operational.
  • Must adhere to the MA-TF1 Photo/Video Review Checklist guidelines.
  • Shall not be shared until all FEMA US&R Teams have demobilized, and must follow any guidance from FEMA or the AHJ.
  • Media that doesn't meet the criteria above may be used for internal training and education.

Distribution Guidelines

  • No media from any category above is shared outside designated channels until approved by the IST PIO or designee.
  • Assigned TIS/Plans personnel establish relationships, communication channels, and deadlines with the IST PIO/designee as soon as possible.
  • All MA-TF1 member media is sent to designated TIS/Plans personnel at the end of every shift unless directed otherwise.
  • TIS/Plans personnel endeavor to send 5–10 photos to the IST PIO/designee by the established deadline, or by end of shift if none is set.
  • Timely and high-value media (successful rescue, humanitarian efforts, unique actions, etc.) is forwarded to the IST PIO/designee as soon as possible.
  • Once approved by the IST PIO/designee, assigned TIS/Plans personnel may share approved media with MA-TF1 personnel, family members, or others on an MA-TF1 PIO distribution list or social media platform.
  • TIS personnel strip metadata before distribution when warranted or requested.
  • Task Force members (deployed and not), family members, and supporting agencies are prohibited from distributing or posting media that hasn't been approved for public distribution or specifically authorized by MA-TF1 leadership.

Demobilization & Post-Deployment

  • All requirements above (unless noted otherwise) are followed through demobilization and dismissal from the Cache.
  • Prior to or during demobilization, TIS/Plans personnel send the MA-TF1 PIO a shared link of any photos not yet reviewed for distribution approval.
  • Once the IST PIO has demobilized, media-related questions/approvals are directed by the MA-TF1 Program Manager and/or PIO, in coordination with the Branch Office as necessary.
  • The Task Force continues to follow federal laws, standard guidelines, high ethical/moral standards, industry best practices, and common sense when determining media release.
  • Adhere to any post-event guidance issued by the IST PIO, FEMA, or AHJ.
  • Except for high-profile, nationally significant, or criminal-act media (or unless prohibited otherwise by FEMA/AHJ), MA-TF1 may destroy/delete deployment media in accordance with Massachusetts and federal record retention requirements.

Member Interview Elements

If MA-TF1 members are interviewed by media during or after a disaster, keep these elements in mind to communicate effectively while maintaining professionalism and sensitivity.

Appearance
Confirm your appearance and the background are in line with the optics you want to convey.
Talking Points
Create brief talking points / soundbites ahead of time.
Accuracy
Ensure all information is accurate and verified to the best of your ability. Avoid speculation or rumors.
Clarity
Speak clearly and concisely, using simple language. Avoid jargon or technical terms the public may not understand.
Empathy
Demonstrate empathy and compassion for those affected. Acknowledge the human impact and express sympathy for victims and families.
Authority
Speak with authority and confidence, conveying your expertise.
Transparency
Be transparent about the current situation, including challenges or limitations. Don't withhold information or downplay severity. Review what you plan to say with the IST PIO, IC, and/or TFL–Program Manager if possible.
Coordination
Coordinate with the MA-TF1 PIO, IST PIO/designee, and response agencies to ensure consistent messaging. Avoid conflicting or contradictory statements.
Respect
Show respect for the dignity and privacy of those affected. Avoid intrusive or insensitive questions; don't disclose personal information without consent.
Media Training
Undergo media training when possible. Practice delivering key messages and handling challenging questions calmly.
Avoid Saying "No Comment"

"No comment" can read as dismissive, uncaring, or evasive — eroding trust and credibility. Use these instead:

  • Acknowledge the question — show you're listening and engaged.
  • Redirect or deflect — if you can't give a detailed response due to confidentiality or an ongoing investigation, shift to an aspect you can discuss. It's OK to say "I cannot provide that information at this time."
  • Offer to follow up — once more information is available or after consulting stakeholders.
  • Bridge to key messages — steer the conversation back to talking points you're prepared to discuss.
  • Express regret or limitations — when appropriate, explain why you can't comment further.

Credentials Required

Access requires a UserID and password. Request credentials from the TIS or Planning Team Manager.
